result.notes: The 0615bc5 fix patched the PRE-PASS's draft check. The MAIN compose leg has the identical bug, unfixed, and it is the leg that does the bulk of the NLI work. Clean sub-lanes (ruled out, no finding): asyncio default to_thread executor is 32 workers on this 128-core box (not binding); OpenRouterClient uses httpx.AsyncClient + await asyncio.sleep everywhere (clean); the entailment judge's httpx client is threading.local (no cross-thread client contention); no sqlite/checkpoint/ev_store writes on the section event loop. Probe script: /tmp/probe_loopblock.py. Env facts from /home/polaris/wt/compose/launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh: PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1, PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CALL_DEADLINE_S=300, PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S=300, PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2, PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY unset.

findings.title: The MAIN compose leg is a sync call on the event loop — same bug as 0615bc5, bigger blast radius
findings.mechanism: `async def _run_section` (:5182) — fanned out concurrently via `_gather_sections_isolated` + `asyncio.Semaphore(PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2)` — calls `_compose_section_per_basket(...)` with NO await and NO asyncio.to_thread. That function is sync (verified_compose.py:3262) and loops over EVERY basket of the section -> `_compose_one_basket` (verified_compose.py:2192) -> `verify_fn` = make_writer_verify_fn(verify_sentence_provenance) -> provenance_generator.py:2558 -> `_get_judge().judge()` -> entailment_judge.py:172 `fut.result(timeout=300)`, which BLOCKS THE CALLING THREAD. The calling thread is the asyncio event loop. So every compose-time NLI POST freezes the whole loop: the other in-flight section cannot progress, and neither can the just-fixed parallel writer pre-pass of any other section. This is the leg that re-verifies every sentence of all 346 baskets, i.e. the dominant NLI cost, and it is 100% serialized. Same on-loop block also covers `_repair_untokened_draft` (:4827), the multicited per-clause verify, and cross_source_synthesis.
findings.evidence:
asyncio.iscoroutinefunction(_compose_section_per_basket) == False; call site at :5468 has no `await`. MEASURED with /tmp/probe_loopblock.py (real _compose_section_per_basket, _compose_one_basket stubbed to a 0.20s blocking judge POST, 8 baskets, 2 sections gathered = production shape):
  PRODUCTION (sync call) wall=3.21s  max_loop_lag=3.20s
  to_thread (fixed)      wall=1.61s  max_loop_lag=0.00s
  (ideal if 2 sections truly parallel: 1.60s)
The loop is starved for the entire duration of compose; the 2-way section parallelism collapses to 1.
findings.fix_sketch: `_vc_composed = await asyncio.to_thread(_compose_section_per_basket, _vc_baskets, evidence_pool, writer_fn=..., verify_fn=..., ...)` at both call sites (:5468, :5514) — exactly the 0615bc5 pattern. Then raise PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS, since section parallelism becomes real for the first time.

findings.title: SYNTH_PRIMARY re-draft blocks the event loop for up to 330 seconds per basket via fut.result()
findings.mechanism: `_make_group_redraft_fn._redraft` is a SYNC closure threaded into `_compose_one_basket`'s bounded repair loop (`_synth_primary_repair_loop`, verified_compose.py:2136 `fresh = redraft_fn(...)`). It does `fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run()))` then `fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0)` — a blocking wait for a FULL GLM-5.2 writer call. Because `_compose_section_per_basket` itself runs on the event loop (finding 1), this `fut.result()` blocks the event loop for the entire writer call, bounded only at call_deadline_s+30 = 300+30 = 330 SECONDS. It fires up to PG_WRITER_REPAIR_MAX (default 2) times per basket, for every basket whose pre-pass draft failed verification. The function's own docstring states it 'runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop'.
findings.evidence:
multi_section_generator.py:4994-4996:
        fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run()))
        try:
            result = str(fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0) or "")
call_deadline_s = _env_float(PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CALL_DEADLINE_S, 120.0); launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh:36 sets it to 300. PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1 (iter5:22) and _vc_redraft_fn is non-None only when _synth_primary_active, so this path IS live. verified_compose.py:1739 _writer_repair_max default 2.
findings.fix_sketch: Make the repair loop async, or (minimal) hoist the whole `_compose_section_per_basket` call off-loop per finding 1 — then _redraft's fut.result() blocks a worker thread instead of the loop. Do NOT leave a 330s .result() reachable from the loop thread.

findings.title: Every NLI judge POST is gated by a process-global BoundedSemaphore(4) — acquired ON the event loop thread
findings.mechanism: `_post_with_total_deadline` opens with `with _acquire_judge_slot():` BEFORE submitting the POST. `acquire_judge_slot` resolves PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, which is set in NO launch script, so it falls to DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4, and does a blocking `sem.acquire()` on a process-global threading.BoundedSemaphore. Two consequences: (a) at most 4 entailment POSTs are in flight process-wide no matter that PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 and PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 — the fixed pre-pass's 12 verify threads immediately queue 8-deep behind a cap of 4, so the 0615bc5 speedup is itself capped; (b) on the compose leg (finding 1) the thread doing sem.acquire() IS the event loop, so slot-queueing time is loop-freeze time on top of POST time.
findings.evidence: Measured under the iter5 env: `resolve_max_concurrency() == 4`, `DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY == 4`. `grep -c PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY launch_compose_gear_iter{1,3,4,5}.sh scripts/run_s5_i3.py` -> 0 in every file. judge_concurrency.py:178 `sem.acquire()` (no timeout on the binding callers).
findings.fix_sketch: Set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY explicitly in the launch script (it was sized at 4 for a 4-host mirror chain during a 429 storm; with the compose leg off-loop, raise it to match PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM and watch 429 rate). Independently: never call acquire_judge_slot from the loop thread.

findings.title: time.sleep() rate-limit backoff (15-60s) inside the judge retry runs on the event loop thread
findings.mechanism: `_EntailmentJudge.judge()` (:740) is fully synchronous. On a retryable fault it computes `_sleep_s = _rate_limit_backoff_s(...)` and calls `time.sleep(_sleep_s)` — not asyncio.sleep. For a 429/503/DNS fault the backoff is floored at PG_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_FLOOR_S=15.0 and capped at PG_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_CAP_S=60.0, with a LARGER retry budget than the ordinary path. Reached from the compose leg (finding 1), that time.sleep executes on the asyncio event loop: one 429 on one sentence freezes the ENTIRE report generation — all sections, all pre-pass writer calls — for 15-60s, repeatedly. The module's own docstring says 429 storms on the mirror-chain lead host are the expected steady state.
findings.evidence: entailment_judge.py:1166 `time.sleep(_sleep_s)` and :1209 `time.sleep(_sleep_s)`, both inside `judge()`'s retry loop. _DEFAULT_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_FLOOR_S = 15.0, _DEFAULT_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_CAP_S = 60.0. judge() is called from verify_sentence_provenance (provenance_generator.py:2558), which the compose leg calls on the loop.
findings.fix_sketch: Getting the compose leg off-loop (finding 1) makes these sleeps block a worker thread, which is correct. Do not leave any path where judge() can be entered from the loop thread.

findings.title: Two serial blocking judge POSTs per sentence (primary + NEUTRAL rescue), both on the loop
findings.mechanism: `verify_sentence_provenance` calls `_get_judge().judge(sentence_clean, combined_span)` at :2558; when that returns NEUTRAL it re-judges against a bounded local window at :2688 with a SECOND `_get_judge().judge(...)`. Both are sequential blocking POSTs (each up to PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S=300s of fut.result). So a sentence that first-NEUTRALs costs 2x the loop-freeze of finding 1, and the two calls cannot overlap with anything.
findings.evidence: provenance_generator.py:2558 `verdict, reason = _get_judge().judge(sentence_clean, combined_span,)`; :2688 `verdict2, reason2 = _get_judge().judge(sentence_clean, local_window_text + _author_metadata_annotation(...))`. Note the rescue call's span differs from the primary's, so judge_verdict_cache (keyed on (model, sentence, span, variant)) cannot serve it from the primary's entry — it is always a real network call on a miss.
findings.fix_sketch: Off-loop per finding 1. Optionally issue the rescue judge only when the primary verdict is NEUTRAL (already the case) but batch/parallelize across sentences once the leg is threaded.

findings.title: Analyst-synthesis deviation screen: sync ThreadPool + fut.result() called from an async def
findings.mechanism: Inside `async def generate_analyst_synthesis` (:640), line :785 calls the SYNC `_apply_synthesis_deviation_screen` (:472) with no await/to_thread. That calls `screen_synthesis_against_baskets`, which builds a `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=_max_inflight())` (default 8) of sentinel-judge calls and blocks on `fut.result(timeout=_remaining)` (analyst_synthesis_deviation_check.py:644). The event loop is frozen for the whole screen. Same bug class; one-shot at report finalization rather than per-basket, so lower total cost, but it stalls any concurrent regen/M-50 gathers.
findings.evidence: analyst_synthesis.py:640 `async def generate_analyst_synthesis`; :785 `cleaned = _apply_synthesis_deviation_screen(` (sync def at :472). analyst_synthesis_deviation_check.py:644 `supported[i] = bool(fut.result(timeout=_remaining))` under `pool = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=_max_inflight())` (_DEFAULT_MAX_INFLIGHT = 8).
findings.fix_sketch: `cleaned, _dev_tel = await asyncio.to_thread(_apply_synthesis_deviation_screen, ...)`.

findings.title: A fresh ThreadPoolExecutor is constructed and torn down for EVERY judge POST
findings.mechanism: `_post_with_total_deadline` does `ex = concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)` per call, submits one POST, then `ex.shutdown(wait=False)`. Over a 346-basket run (thousands of sentences x up to 2 judge calls x retries) this creates/destroys thousands of OS threads purely to get a timeout on a blocking POST. On the timeout path `shutdown(wait=False)` also abandons the worker, so a wedged 300s POST leaks a live thread that still holds nothing but still exists. Pure overhead, not a serializer.
findings.evidence: entailment_judge.py:152 `ex = concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)`; :172 `return fut.result(timeout=total_s)`; :180 `ex.shutdown(wait=False)` in the finally.
findings.fix_sketch: Use one module-level bounded executor (sized to the judge slot cap), or drop the executor entirely once the leg is off-loop and rely on httpx's explicit Timeout + the existing read-stall.

result.notes:
REAL EXECUTION GRAPH (run_s5_i3.py -> _run_section, per compose gear iter3/4/5):

main() [run_s5_i3.py:588-597] -> asyncio.gather over 5 section coroutines, gated by sec_sema = Semaphore(PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS) [:491] = 2 (iter4/5) or 3 (iter3).
Per section (_run_section, multi_section_generator.py:5182):
  [PARALLEL - the only real fan-out] abstractive_pre_pass :5448 -> asyncio.wait over per-basket tasks, Semaphore(24). This is what commit 0615bc5 fixed. Measured: 14 baskets / 381s.
  [BARRIER 1 - SYNC ON THE EVENT LOOP] _compose_section_per_basket :5468 (and :5514) -- plain call, NO await, NO to_thread. It is `def` (verified with inspect), loops `for basket in section_baskets` (verified_compose.py:3325) and calls verify_fn(sentence, scoped_pool) (:2222, :2583, :2981, :3071, :3192) + nested companion-unit loop (:3414) -> verify_sentence_provenance -> entailment_judge.judge() -> _post_with_total_deadline -> fut.result() -> BLOCKS THE EVENT LOOP THREAD. Same bug class as 0615bc5, at the section level, NOT fixed.
  [BARRIER 2] _repair_untokened_draft :5575 -- sync def (mg:4827), per-sentence loop calling the same blocking verify_fn, also on the loop thread.
  [BARRIER 3] _coherence_rewrite :5591 -- async, but its body (section_polish.py:271-283) loops sentences calling SYNC verify_sentence_provenance -> blocking judge, on the loop thread.
  [BARRIER 4] strict_verify :5667 -- correctly to_thread'd, but INTERNALLY SERIAL: PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY is unset in every launch_compose_gear_*.sh and never setdefault'ed in run_s5_i3.py, so _parallel_verify_workers() returns 1 (provenance_generator.py:3383-3392) and the judge loop takes the serial path at :3664-3676.
  [BARRIER 5] repair_dropped_section_sentences :5693 -- `for sv in dropped:` with `await repair_sentence()` inside (sentence_repair.py:408/428), up to 10 LLM calls strictly one at a time, plus blocking verify_sentence_provenance at :519.
  [BARRIER 6] regen path: second _call_section :5775 + second serial strict_verify :5804.

CONSEQUENCE: the ONLY parallel phase is the pre-pass; everything after it is one blocking chain, and because barriers 1-3 run ON the event loop, a section in its tail freezes EVERY other section's async writer to zero throughput. Achieved global concurrency during the tail = ~1 request, with PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=30.

MEASURED (live run /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log, PID 966951):
- Section 2 (15 baskets): pre-pass done 14:02:38 (14/14 in 381s); section DONE 14:38:32. Serial tail = 2154s = 85% of the 2536s section wall, for 6 verified sentences.
- Section 3 pre-pass ran INSIDE that window and drafted 0/13 baskets in 1700s -> "pre-pass WALL-DEADLINE 1700s hit: ABANDONING 13/13 ... 0 drafted before the wall" (log:10128, 14:36:28). Section 2 finished at 14:38:32; the K-span recovery pass then drafted 13/13 in 315s (log:10480, 14:41:43). Zero -> full throughput the instant the sibling's sync tail released the loop. That is the barrier, measured.
- Earlier in the same run: a 12-basket pre-pass took 1637s with "writer stall reconnect budget exhausted" while its sibling was in its tail; the sibling did 14 baskets in 424s when the loop was free.
- Global POST rate: pre-pass window 0.27/s; post-draft window 0.155/s, median inter-POST gap 5.0s = strictly one judge call in flight.
- 3713 OpenRouter POSTs total; 1261 async generate() calls => ~2450 judge POSTs, essentially all issued serially from blocking sync code.

PROJECTION: section 4 = "Additional Corroborated Findings" with 304-326 of the 346 baskets (88-94% of the work) in ONE coroutine. At section 2's measured tail rate (~2154s / 15 baskets = ~143s/basket of serial verify), its tail alone projects to ~12 hours, during which no other section can make progress. Sections are not the unit of parallelism for this run - one section IS the run.

findings.title: _compose_section_per_basket is a SYNC blocking basket loop called with no await/to_thread from the async _run_section - it serializes every per-basket NLI judge AND freezes the whole event loop (all other sections)
findings.mechanism: _run_section is `async def`. At :5468 it calls `_vc_composed = _compose_section_per_basket(...)` as a plain function call. inspect.iscoroutinefunction(_compose_section_per_basket) == False. Its body loops `for basket in (section_baskets or [])` (verified_compose.py:3325) and, per basket, calls _compose_one_basket / compose_basket_multicited_synth_primary / compose_companion_figure_units (nested member x sentence-unit loop at :3414), each of which calls the SYNC verify_fn(sentence, scoped_pool) (verified_compose.py:2222, 2583, 2981, 3071, 3192) -> make_writer_verify_fn -> verify_sentence_provenance (provenance_generator.py:2154, sync) -> entailment_judge.judge() (sync) -> _post_with_total_deadline -> fut.result(timeout) which BLOCKS THE CALLING THREAD. The calling thread is the asyncio event loop. So (a) all per-basket verification in a section runs one judge POST at a time, and (b) for the entire duration the event loop is frozen, so every OTHER section's writer tasks, timeouts and HTTP callbacks make ZERO progress. This is exactly the 0615bc5 bug class, one level up, unfixed.
findings.evidence: inspect probe: _compose_section_per_basket coroutine=False, _compose_one_basket coroutine=False, compose_companion_figure_units coroutine=False, verify_sentence_provenance coroutine=False; call site printed as `_vc_composed = _compose_section_per_basket(` with no await/to_thread (both sites). LIVE LOG PROOF (/workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log): section 2's pre-pass finished 14:02:38 and it entered this sync stage; section 3, running concurrently, drafted 0 of 13 baskets for the next 1700s -> log line 10128 'pre-pass WALL-DEADLINE 1700s hit: ABANDONING 13/13 still-pending basket task(s) ... 0 drafted before the wall'. Section 2 DONE at 14:38:32 (log:10250); section 3's recovery pass then drafted 13/13 in 315s (log:10480, 14:41:43). Zero throughput while the sibling held the loop, full throughput 2 minutes after it released. Global POST rate in the tail window: 0.155/s, median inter-POST gap 5.0s = one request in flight, with PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=30.
findings.fix_sketch: Wrap both call sites in `await asyncio.to_thread(_compose_section_per_basket, ...)` (unblocks the loop immediately, same as 0615bc5), then go further: make the per-basket loop fan out - either an async _compose_section_per_basket that gathers per-basket coroutines under a semaphore, or a bounded ThreadPoolExecutor over baskets with a copied contextvars context (the pattern already written for strict_verify at provenance_generator.py:3676-3718). Baskets are independent; verdicts are order-independent and are reassembled by index.

findings.title: The post-draft tail is ~85-88% of every section's wall clock and has NO fan-out at all - the parallel writer pre-pass is only ~12-15% of the work
findings.mechanism: The execution graph per section is: parallel pre-pass (:5448, Semaphore(24)) -> then a strictly serial chain of five blocking/serial stages: _compose_section_per_basket (:5468), _repair_untokened_draft (:5575), _coherence_rewrite (:5591), strict_verify (:5667), sentence repair (:5693), optional regen + a SECOND strict_verify (:5775/:5804). None of these stages pipelines with the next, and none of them fans out over sentences/baskets. So raising PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY / PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM can only ever compress the 12-15% slice; the other 85% is immune to every concurrency knob in the launch scripts.
findings.evidence: Section 2 (15 baskets): '[abstractive_writer] pre-pass complete: 14/14 baskets drafted (elapsed=381.5s)' at 14:02:38; '[section 2] DONE 2536s ... sentences_verified=6' at 14:38:32. Tail = 2154s = 85% of the section wall to verify ~9 sentences. Same shape for sections 0/1 (pre-pass complete 12:11:19, DONE ~12:54; tail ~2560s of a 2945s wall = 87%). Log totals: 3713 OpenRouter POSTs vs 1261 async generate() calls => ~2450 judge POSTs, virtually all issued one-at-a-time from the sync tail.
findings.fix_sketch: Parallelize inside the tail (see the other findings: basket loop, strict_verify workers, coherence re-verify, sentence repair) and/or pipeline it: start the next section's pre-pass while the previous section's verify tail runs - which becomes automatic once the tail stops blocking the event loop.

findings.title: 94% of the baskets live in ONE section coroutine ('Additional Corroborated Findings', 304-326 of 346), and it is scheduled LAST behind a Semaphore(2)
findings.mechanism: plans=4 -> 5 sections. Sections 0-3 carry 15-18 baskets each; section 4 carries 304-326. sec_sema = Semaphore(PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS) = 2 (iter4/iter5 launchers) and tasks are created in plan order, so section 4 only acquires the semaphore after two of the four small sections complete - i.e. it STARTS ~50-90 minutes into the run. Because the section is the unit of parallelism and its whole compose+verify tail is the single-threaded loop of finding #1, 94% of the work has an achieved parallelism of exactly 1. PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS cannot help: there is no second big section to overlap with.
findings.evidence: compose.log: '[load] question_len=2045 evidence_pool=996 cp3_baskets=346 plans=4'; '[section 4] START title=Additional Corroborated Findings ev_ids=304' printed only at 14:38:32, i.e. 90 minutes after the run started at 13:08:54, immediately after '[section 2] DONE 2536s'. Sections 0-3 have ev_ids=18/15/15/17. Extrapolating section 2's measured serial tail (2154s / 15 baskets = ~143s per basket of blocking judge calls) to 304 baskets projects a ~12h tail for section 4 alone - matching the launcher comment in launch_compose_gear_iter3.sh ('section 4 = 276 baskets @ concurrency 4 -> ~4h makespan').
findings.fix_sketch: Two independent fixes: (1) shard the mega-section's baskets into N chunks composed by N concurrent tasks (the basket loop is already order-stable and dedup is done on the concatenated list, so chunking + in-order reassembly is safe); (2) schedule sections longest-first (sort tasks by len(section.ev_ids) descending) so the mega-section starts at t=0 and overlaps with all the small ones instead of being tail-scheduled.

findings.title: strict_verify's per-sentence entailment loop runs SERIAL because PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY is never set - the bounded thread pool exists and is dead code in production
findings.mechanism: _parallel_verify_workers() returns 1 when PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY is unset/empty (:3390-3392). strict_verify then takes the serial branch at :3666-3676 ('if _verify_workers < 2 ... byte-identical to the legacy loop'), running one blocking entailment-judge POST per sentence, sequentially. The ThreadPoolExecutor path at :3678-3718 - which already handles contextvars copying and cost-ledger reconciliation - is never entered. strict_verify is invoked twice per section (:5667 and, on regen, :5804). grep shows PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY appears in NO launch_compose_gear_*.sh and in no os.environ.setdefault in run_s5_i3.py (which does setdefault 5 other flags at :342-359).
findings.evidence: grep -rn 'PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY' launch_compose_gear_iter*.sh scripts/run_s5_i3.py -> no matches. Code: `raw = os.getenv("PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY", "").strip(); if not raw: return 1`. Judge POST latency measured at ~5s median in the tail, so a 20-sentence section pays ~100s serially per strict_verify pass, x2 passes.
findings.fix_sketch: export PG_PARALLEL_VERIFY=8 (or setdefault it in run_s5_i3.py next to the other Fix-3/Fix-6 setdefaults at :342-359). Zero code change; the parallel path is already written, tested and cost-reconciled.

findings.title: coherence_rewrite_section is an async coroutine that calls the SYNC blocking verify_sentence_provenance once per sentence in a for-loop - blocking the event loop again
findings.mechanism: `async def coherence_rewrite_section` is awaited from _run_section at multi_section_generator.py:5591. After the single rewrite LLM call it does `for orig, new in zip(sentences, new_sentences):` (:271) and inside calls `res = verify_sentence_provenance(new, evidence_pool)` (:283) - the SYNC verifier, which runs the entailment judge over a blocking fut.result(). No await, no to_thread. So the whole re-verify sweep is serial AND runs on the event loop thread, freezing every other section for N_sentences x ~5s. Default-ON (PG_SECTION_COHERENCE_PASS kill-switch defaults on via _flag_on(default_on=True)).
findings.evidence: Source read: line 283 `res = verify_sentence_provenance(new, evidence_pool)` inside the `for orig, new in zip(...)` loop at :271; inspect confirms verify_sentence_provenance is a plain def. Called at multi_section_generator.py:5591 inside `async def _run_section`.
findings.fix_sketch: Hoist the whole re-verify loop into one `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` call, and inside it use a bounded ThreadPoolExecutor over the changed sentences (only the sentences where new != orig and the token multiset matches actually need a judge call).

findings.title: repair_dropped_section_sentences: classic await-inside-a-for-loop - up to 10 repair LLM calls strictly one at a time, plus a blocking verify per repair
findings.mechanism: `for sv in dropped:` at :408 with `outcome, repaired_text, in_tok, out_tok = await repair_sentence(...)` at :428 inside it. Each repair is a full LLM round-trip (own client, own generate) and they are awaited sequentially, so N dropped sentences cost N x latency instead of max(latency). Then at :519 `re_verify = verify_sentence_provenance(...)` - the sync blocking judge - is called from this async function, so each repair also freezes the event loop for the duration of an NLI POST. Reached from _run_section:5693 on every section that drops a sentence (all of them: sections 0-3 dropped 2-9 sentences each in the live run). An AST scan of the whole generator package found this as the only genuine accidental await-in-loop; every other hit is a bounded retry loop.
findings.evidence: AST scan output: 'src/polaris_graph/generator/sentence_repair.py:408 for-loop -> await repair_sentence at :428'. Live log shows dropped counts per section: sec0 dropped=5, sec1 dropped=5, sec2 dropped=3, sec3 dropped=2, plus 9 and 3 in earlier attempts - the repair cap is MAX 10 per section.
findings.fix_sketch: asyncio.gather the repair_sentence() calls over the dropped list under a small semaphore (results are per-sentence independent; the deterministic order requirement is satisfied by reassembling by index), and to_thread the :519 re-verify.

findings.title: The same sentence is judged by the NLI entailment judge up to 4 times, and 3 of those passes are serial
findings.mechanism: Pass 1: abstractive_pre_pass verifies each drafted sentence via writer_verify_fn (parallel, post-0615bc5). Pass 2: _compose_section_per_basket re-verifies the SAME precomputed draft sentence at verified_compose.py:2222 (serial, blocking). Pass 3: coherence_rewrite_section re-verifies every rewritten sentence at section_polish.py:283 (serial, blocking). Pass 4: strict_verify re-verifies every sentence again at :5667 (serial thread). So a section that keeps 12 sentences issues ~4x12 judge POSTs, ~36 of them one-at-a-time. This is why the log shows ~2450 judge POSTs for a run that kept only a few dozen sentences.
findings.evidence: 3713 total OpenRouter POSTs vs 1261 'generate completed' (writer/coherence/repair) lines in compose.log => ~2450 judge POSTs, against section outputs of 6/7/6/10/12 verified sentences. Code paths for the 4 verify sites read and cited above.
findings.fix_sketch: Cache judge verdicts by (sentence_text, span_key) for the lifetime of a section - pass 2 re-judges a sentence that pass 1 already passed with the identical scoped pool, and pass 4 re-judges an unmodified sentence from pass 2/3. A dict memo on the writer_verify_fn closure would eliminate most of the duplicate POSTs with no faithfulness change (identical inputs -> identical verdict).

findings.title: A new OpenRouter client (new httpx pool, new TLS handshake) is constructed per call, and the 'global' concurrency semaphore is rebuilt whenever the running loop identity changes
findings.mechanism: `if _LLM_SEMAPHORE is None or _LLM_SEMAPHORE_LOOP is not running_loop: _LLM_SEMAPHORE = asyncio.Semaphore(cap)` - because the blocking judge path runs off the loop (and sync callers see running_loop=None), the semaphore object is repeatedly REPLACED mid-run, so PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=30 is not a stable global cap; waiters and holders can be on different semaphore objects. Separately, each writer/coherence/repair call builds and closes its own client (abstractive_writer.py:656, section_polish.py:214), so every call pays TCP+TLS setup - consistent with the ConnectTimeout storms in the log.
findings.evidence: compose.log: 1408 'OpenRouter client initialized' lines and 306 '[llm_provider] Concurrency semaphore (re)initialized: max=30' lines in a single run; interleaved 'writer call transport-disconnect (ConnectTimeout)' bursts (e.g. 9 in one second at 08:39:50).
findings.fix_sketch: Hold one client (one httpx connection pool) per process and one semaphore per loop created at startup; do not re-init on every construction. Flagging for the client/transport lane - it is adjacent to my seam findings, not the primary barrier.

result.lane: THE NLI JUDGE / VERIFIER — the high-call-volume half
result.notes:
HEADLINE: the missing `await` was NOT the cap. I measured the compose cost ledger (/workspace/compose_wt/logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl) across the commit boundary of 0615bc5 (2026-07-11 14:23:29 UTC):

  PRE-FIX  (11:12 -> 14:23):  2335 judge calls, wall 11243s, serial 19371s -> achieved judge concurrency 1.72
  POST-FIX (14:23 -> end):     209 judge calls, wall  1981s, serial  1935s -> achieved judge concurrency 0.98

The judge is STILL running at concurrency ~1 AFTER the await fix. The fix moved the abstractive_writer PRE-PASS off the event loop, but the pre-pass only precomputes drafts; the AUTHORITATIVE verify gate that decides what actually ships lives in verified_compose.py, which contains ZERO asyncio and ZERO ThreadPoolExecutor (grep count = 0 for `asyncio|ThreadPoolExecutor|async def` in that whole 5000-line file) and iterates baskets and sentences in plain serial for-loops. That is finding #1 and it fully explains the measured 0.98.

VOLUME (compose ledger): 5347 entailment_judge calls vs 2246 generate calls -> the judge is 70% of all LLM calls, 2.4x the writer. Serial judge sum = 47,425s = 13.2h; run wall = 48,644s. The judge IS the compose wall clock (97.5% of it).

BATCHING: OpenRouter has no batch endpoint, but nothing stops packing N (span, sentence) pairs into one prompt. Mean judge call = 1008 input tokens / 245 output tokens against a max_tokens of 131072 — a ~1k-token payload paying an 8.9s (compose) / 19.7s (DR sweep) round trip. _draft_passes_wrapper already holds the whole sentence list. 6-way batching = ~6x fewer POSTs.

CACHE — CLEAN LANE, reporting honestly: I checked for the key-mismatch hypothesis and there is NO bug. entailment_judge.judge() (:755-762) computes `variant` once and threads the identical (model, sentence, span, variant) tuple to both `_verdict_cache.get` and `.put`; judge_verdict_cache.make_key() whitespace-normalizes and sha256s consistently. The pre-pass (make_writer_verify_fn -> base_verify -> _basket_scoped_pool) and the authoritative loop (_compose_one_basket -> same base_verify -> same _basket_scoped_pool) produce identical (sentence, span) inputs, so the second pass SHOULD hit. reset_cache() is never called on any compose path. The cache is correct. Its two real (lesser) defects are no single-flight dedup and zero production observability — reported as a MEDIUM, not inflated.

I did not print or read the OpenRouter API key at any point.

findings.title: The authoritative NLI verify gate is a fully SERIAL sync loop — judge concurrency ceiling is PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS (=2), not 12/16/48. This is what the await fix did NOT fix.
findings.mechanism: verified_compose.py:3325 `for basket in (section_baskets or []):` is a plain serial for-loop inside a plain sync `def _compose_section_per_basket`. Each iteration calls _compose_one_basket (:2192), which at :2221-2222 runs `for sentence in split_into_sentences(draft): res = verify_fn(sentence, scoped_pool)` — another plain serial for-loop. verify_fn -> verify_sentence_provenance -> provenance_generator.py:2558 `_get_judge().judge(...)` -> a BLOCKING NLI POST (mean 8.9s). The ENTIRE verified_compose.py module contains zero asyncio, zero ThreadPoolExecutor, zero `async def` (grep count = 0). So every sentence that actually ships is NLI-judged one-at-a-time, basket-after-basket. The only concurrency above this is scripts/run_s5_i3.py:491 `asyncio.Semaphore(PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2)` — 2 sections. The judge's hard concurrency ceiling is therefore 2, no matter what PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 / PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 say. The abstractive_writer async pre-pass (now correctly off-loop) only PRECOMPUTES drafts — abstractive_writer.py:1113 states the sync writer_fn _compose_one_basket reads is 'a pure dict lookup'. Parallelizing the writer while leaving the verifier serial parallelizes 30% of the calls and leaves 70% single-file.
findings.evidence:
MEASURED from /workspace/compose_wt/logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl, split at commit 0615bc5 (2026-07-11 14:23:29 UTC):
  PRE-FIX  11:12->14:23: 2335 judge calls, wall 11243s, serial-sum 19371s -> ACHIEVED JUDGE CONCURRENCY 1.72
  POST-FIX 14:23->end  :  209 judge calls, wall  1981s, serial-sum  1935s -> ACHIEVED JUDGE CONCURRENCY 0.98
The await fix did not move judge concurrency off 1. Measured values 0.98/1.72 sit exactly where a serial-per-section loop with PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2 predicts (ceiling 2).
Call-volume proof that the judge is the half that matters: ledger call_type counts = entailment_judge 5347 vs generate 2246 (judge = 70% of all LLM calls). Judge serial-sum 47,425s (13.2h) against a 48,644s run wall = 97.5% of the compose wall clock.
`grep -c 'asyncio|ThreadPoolExecutor|async def' src/polaris_graph/generator/verified_compose.py` -> 0
findings.fix_sketch: Parallelize the two loops. (a) In _compose_one_basket (:2221), verify the draft's sentences through a bounded ThreadPoolExecutor with a copied contextvars context — the pattern already exists and is battle-tested at provenance_generator.py:3716 (_verify_in_context / _parent_ctx.copy().run). (b) Run _compose_section_per_basket's basket loop (:3325) over a bounded pool too, or hoist it into the writer's existing async gather. (c) Raise PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS. Note verdicts are order-independent and results are reassembled by index, exactly as provenance_generator's parallel path already does.

findings.title: Process-global side-judge semaphore hard-caps ALL NLI POSTs at 4 in flight — never set in any launch script, and will become the binding cap the moment finding #1 is fixed
findings.mechanism: judge_concurrency.py:55 `DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4`. entailment_judge.py:155 wraps EVERY judge POST in `with _acquire_judge_slot():`, which acquires one slot of a process-global threading.BoundedSemaphore sized by resolve_max_concurrency() (:81). PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY is not set in launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh, iter4, iter3, or scripts/run_s5_i3.py -> resolves to 4. The semaphore is SHARED with credibility_judge_caller and semantic_conflict_detector, so those consume compose's judge slots too. This cap was tuned for a 20-way credibility burst against a 4-host mirror chain ([friendli, novita, z-ai, phala]) — it is not a compose-throughput number. The credibility pass gets a phase override (multi_section_generator.py:9527, credibility_pass_concurrency) that raises its cap; the compose-time entailment verify has NO override, so it runs at 4 forever. Consequence: even if you parallelize the serial verify loop to the writer's 12, the judge still admits only 4 POSTs at a time.
findings.evidence:
PROVEN with a probe under the exact iter-5 env slate (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12, PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16, PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY unset):
  resolve_max_concurrency() = 4
  launched 48 concurrent threads through the real acquire_judge_slot() chokepoint
  PEAK simultaneous judge POSTs = 4   (wall 3.00s = 48*0.25s/4, exactly the cap)
Also measured in /workspace/POLARIS/logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl: one DR session with 2170 judge calls shows achieved judge concurrency 3.94 — that session sat pinned at the semaphore ceiling for 8208s.
findings.fix_sketch: export PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=16 (or 24) in launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh. The mirror chain is 4 hosts with burst-spread ON (judge_burst_spread default 'spread'), so 16 in flight = ~4 POSTs/host — well within provider capacity and far below the ~32-simultaneous burst that originally 429-stormed friendli. Alternatively set PG_JUDGE_BURST_SPREAD=lb (judge_burst_spread.py:22) to drop the single-host pin and let OpenRouter load-balance across all glm-5.2 endpoints — the config file itself documents this as the D8-Judge cure.

findings.title: A hung judge POST holds a semaphore slot (25% of global judge capacity) for 300s — and PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_DEADLINE_RETRIES is never wired, so one sentence can burn 900s of slot-time
findings.mechanism: _post_with_total_deadline (:139) does `with _acquire_judge_slot():` at :155 and then blocks on `fut.result(timeout=total_s)` at :172 — the semaphore slot is held for the ENTIRE total deadline, not just for productive work. launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh:38 and run_s5_i3.py:357 both set PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S=300, DOUBLE the 150s code default (:136). With the cap of 4 (finding #2), ONE trickle-hung POST removes 25% of the entire pipeline's verify capacity for 5 minutes. Worse: _total_deadline_retries (:953) is `min(_retries, PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_DEADLINE_RETRIES)` and PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_DEADLINE_RETRIES is unset everywhere, so it defaults to _DEFAULT_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_DEADLINE_RETRIES = _DEFAULT_ENTAILMENT_RETRIES = 2 (:200) -> up to 3 attempts x 300s = 900s of slot-holding for a SINGLE sentence before it fail-closed drops. The code comment at :195-197 explicitly says 'the run slate sets PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_DEADLINE_RETRIES=1 to apply the fix' — grep shows NO launch script or run slate sets it. The A2 fix is un-wired in production.
findings.evidence:
COMPOSE ledger: max single judge call = 619.4s (a 300s timeout + backoff + a slow retry — the ledger `started` clock at :793 precedes the retry loop). Real compose log /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log: 4x 'entailment judge retryable fault ... total_deadline_exceeded_300s', 5x blank_200, 12x 'Client (has been closed)'.
TAIL COST (DR ledger, 9469 judge calls): calls >150s = 288 (3.0% of calls) but consume 61,228s = 32.8% of ALL judge seconds. Calls >60s = 830 (8.8%) consume 57.3% of all judge seconds. The slowest 10% of judge calls carry 60.9% of total judge time. Every one of those seconds is holding 1 of only 4 global slots.
findings.fix_sketch: Set PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_DEADLINE_RETRIES=1 in launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh (the value the code comment says the slate is supposed to carry), and drop PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S back toward 120-150s — measured p90 is 13-14s and p50 is 5.4s, so a 300s wall is 20x the p90 and buys almost nothing but slot-blocking. Independently: release the semaphore slot before the retry backoff sleeps (it already is) AND consider not holding it across the full fut.result deadline for a call already known to be trickling.

findings.title: Judge is called ONE (span, sentence) pair per HTTP POST — no batching — even where the caller already holds the whole sentence list
findings.mechanism: entailment_judge.py:830-841 builds json_body with exactly one user message containing one SPAN and one SENTENCE (_ENTAILMENT_PROMPT at :584 is single-pair by construction). Every rendered sentence therefore costs one full OpenRouter round trip with reasoning effort='high' (:270). The natural batch site already exists: abstractive_writer.py:683-684 `for sentence in sentences: res = writer_verify_fn(sentence, scoped_pool)` — _draft_passes_wrapper has the ENTIRE draft's sentence list in hand and issues them one at a time; same for verified_compose.py:2221. The per-call payload is tiny relative to the latency: mean input 1008 tokens, mean output 245 tokens, against max_tokens=131072 (:267-268). So ~1k tokens of work is paying a full 8.9s (compose) / 19.7s (DR) TTFT+queue+reasoning-warmup round trip. Packing N pairs into one prompt and returning a JSON array of N verdicts is fully supported by the chat-completions API, keeps temperature=0.0 idempotency, and cuts POST count (and thus semaphore pressure from finding #2) by N.
findings.evidence: COMPOSE ledger call_type counts: entailment_judge = 5347, generate = 2246. The judge issues 2.4x the writer's HTTP calls and is 70% of all LLM traffic. Per-call stats (compose): mean 8.9s, p50 5.4s, p90 14.1s; mean input_tokens 1008, mean output_tokens 245, max_tokens configured 131072. DR ledger (9469 calls): mean 19.7s, serial-sum 51.8 HOURS. At 6 pairs/call the POST count for a 346-basket run drops ~6x and each batch costs roughly one round-trip instead of six.
findings.fix_sketch: Add a `judge_batch(pairs: list[tuple[str,str]]) -> list[tuple[str,str]]` to _EntailmentJudge: one prompt enumerating N SPAN/SENTENCE pairs, returning a JSON array of N verdict objects; reuse _extract_first_json_object's fail-closed discipline per element and fall back to per-pair calls if the array length mismatches (never silently pad — that would fail OPEN). Wire it at abstractive_writer.py:683 and verified_compose.py:2221, both of which already have the full sentence list. Keep the existing verdict cache in front of it (check cache for each pair, batch only the misses).

findings.title: Rate-limit backoff FLOOR is 15s x 5 retries — a single 429'd sentence can sleep up to 300s, and in the serial compose loop that stalls the whole section
findings.mechanism: _DEFAULT_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_FLOOR_S = 15.0 (:217), cap 60.0 (:219), _DEFAULT_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_RETRIES = 5 (:221). _rate_limit_backoff_s (:488) guarantees the delay is ALWAYS >= floor ('The result is always >= floor'). On a 429 / 502/503/504 / DNS blip / rate_limit_200 envelope, the retry loop does `time.sleep(_sleep_s)` at :1166 and :1195 with _sleep_s in [15s, 60s], up to 5 times => up to ~300s of pure sleep for ONE sentence's verdict. That sleep happens on the calling thread. Because the authoritative verify loop is serial (finding #1), that thread IS the section — so a single rate-limited sentence stalls every remaining basket in that section behind it. None of PG_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_FLOOR_S / _CAP_S / _RETRIES is set in any launch script, so the 15s floor is live.
findings.evidence: Code: entailment_judge.py:217 floor=15.0, :221 retries=5, :505-508 `if capped_exp <= floor: return floor` (the floor is unconditional). Observed in production: /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter3/compose.log contains 'entailment judge retryable fault ... rate_limit_200'. The iter-5 launcher's own header comment documents 429 self-contention ('repeated OpenRouter 429 backoff + a 3-min stall') as the reason it CUT concurrency from 30->16 — i.e. the run is already living in the 429 regime where this 15s floor fires.
findings.fix_sketch: Set PG_ENTAILMENT_RATE_LIMIT_FLOOR_S=2 (honor Retry-After when the server sends it — _extract_retry_after_s at :452 already does, and that path is correct; it is the header-less exponential FLOOR that is over-tuned). Keep the retry count. Combined with the batching fix (#4), 6x fewer POSTs also means 6x less 429 pressure at the source.

findings.title: Verdict cache is correct (no key mismatch) but has no single-flight dedup and zero production observability
findings.mechanism: I explicitly tested the 'key mismatch makes hit rate ~0' hypothesis and it is FALSE — reporting the clean result. judge() at :755-762 resolves `variant = _active_prompt_variant()` ONCE and threads the identical (model, sentence, span, variant) tuple into both _verdict_cache.get(:756) and .put(:762); make_key (judge_verdict_cache.py:96) whitespace-normalizes + sha256s both sides consistently; reset_cache() has ZERO non-test callers (grep) so it is never nuked mid-run; and the pre-pass (make_writer_verify_fn -> base_verify -> _basket_scoped_pool) and the authoritative loop (_compose_one_basket -> same base_verify -> same _basket_scoped_pool) feed byte-identical (sentence, span) inputs, so the second pass DOES hit. The two real defects are lesser: (a) NO single-flight — get() at :756 and put() at :762 are separate lock acquisitions with a network call in between, so K threads judging the same (sentence, span) concurrently ALL miss and ALL POST, wasting K-1 of the 4 scarce semaphore slots; (b) judge_verdict_cache.stats() has zero production callers, so the hit rate is unobservable — nobody can tell whether the cache is working without attaching a debugger.
findings.evidence: Read entailment_judge.py:755-763 (variant computed once, passed to both get and put) and judge_verdict_cache.py:96-113 (make_key deterministic over the same 4-tuple). `grep -rn 'reset_cache|judge_verdict_cache' src/ scripts/` returns no production caller that clears the entailment cache and no caller of stats(). Confirmed pre-pass and authoritative loop use the same _basket_scoped_pool(basket, evidence_pool) -> identical judge inputs -> real hits. Cache is NOT the bug; the serial loop (#1) and the semaphore (#2) are.
findings.fix_sketch: (a) Add single-flight: a per-key threading.Event / futures map so the first thread POSTs and the rest await its result — recovers up to 3 of the 4 slots during any duplicate burst. (b) Log judge_verdict_cache.stats() at section boundaries so the hit rate is a number in the run report instead of a guess.

result.reason:
REFUTED. The code reading is correct (the per-call ThreadPoolExecutor at entailment_judge.py:152 is real and IS reached — PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT=enforce in every launch_compose_gear_*.sh, judge() -> _post_with_total_deadline at :999), but every cost assertion is false or negligible.

(1) MEASURED OVERHEAD IS 150us/CALL. Benchmarked the exact pattern (construct executor -> submit -> fut.result(timeout=) -> shutdown(wait=False)) over 5000 iterations: 149.6 us per call. The file's own comment (:135) puts a real GLM-5.2 NLI call at 6-40s. The executor is therefore 0.0025% of the thing it wraps. At an aggressive upper bound of 20,000 judge POSTs for a 346-basket run that is 3 SECONDS of CPU total, against tens of hours of network wall time.

(2) THE THREAD-LEAK CLAIM IS FACTUALLY WRONG — I TESTED IT. Ran 200 forced-timeout cycles reproducing the exact timeout path (blocking worker, fut.result times out, client.close(), ex.shutdown(wait=False)). Thread count: 1 before, 1 after. Zero leak. Mechanically: shutdown(wait=False) STILL enqueues the None sentinel into the work queue (it only declines to JOIN), and client.close() at :175 force-closes the socket so the wedged httpx read raises, the worker drains the sentinel, and the thread exits. The "wedged 300s POST leaks a live thread" scenario is precisely what the client.close() line exists to prevent, and it works.

(3) IT IS DOMINATED BY THE LINE DIRECTLY ABOVE IT. _post_with_total_deadline opens with `with _acquire_judge_slot():` (entailment_judge.py:155) — a process-global BoundedSemaphore (judge_concurrency.py, DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4). No compose launch script sets PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, so it defaults to 4 in-flight judge POSTs process-wide, shared across entailment + credibility + semantic_conflict. The executor is constructed INSIDE that semaphore. Any throughput discussion at this file:line is about the semaphore, not the 150us of thread churn beneath it.

(4) judge() also front-runs a verdict idempotency cache (judge_verdict_cache, :740), cutting actual POST count below the "thousands x 2 x retries" the claim assumes.

The finding is a true observation about the code with a false conclusion about impact. It would send the team to the right file and the wrong line.
result.real_impact: ~150 microseconds per judge POST (measured, 5000-iteration benchmark), i.e. 0.0025% of the 6-40s POST it wraps. Across an entire 346-basket run at an aggressive 20,000 judge POSTs: ~3 seconds of CPU, total, spread over a multi-hour run. Zero threads leaked (measured: 200 forced-timeout cycles, thread count unchanged at baseline). Not a throughput cap; not worth fixing. The REAL cap co-located at this call site is the process-global PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY semaphore (default 4, unset in all compose launch scripts) at entailment_judge.py:155 / judge_concurrency.py:57, which bounds ALL judge POSTs to 4 in flight regardless of PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 — that deserves its own investigation and must not be conflated with this refuted finding.

result.reason:
REFUTED — the code path is never reached by compose, and is triple-gated even in the report path.

1) NOT ON THE COMPOSE ENTRYPOINT. Every launcher (/home/polaris/wt/compose/launch_compose_gear_iter1..5.sh) runs `python -u scripts/run_s5_i3.py`. /home/polaris/wt/compose/scripts/run_s5_i3.py imports ONLY `SectionPlan, _run_section, _GAP_STUB_SENTENCE, _NO_EVIDENCE_GAP_STUB_SENTENCE, _SECTION_FAILED_GAP_STUB_SENTENCE` from multi_section_generator (:367-375) and drives `_run_section` (multi_section_generator.py:5182) itself. 2) EVEN IN THE FULL REPORT PATH IT IS GATED OFF. multi_section_generator.py:10966-10970 requires `not partial_mode and not suppress_analyst_synthesis and analyst_synth_enabled and research_plan is None`. On-mode (research_plan present) demotes the block by design (comment at :10956).

3) EVEN IF ENTERED, IT RETURNS BEFORE THE SCREEN. analyst_synthesis.py:686-692: `PG_ANALYST_SYNTHESIS_EMITTER_FAILCLOSED` defaults ON and `_promote_mode_active()` is False (PG_ANALYST_SYNTHESIS_PROMOTE_GROUNDED defaults "0"; no compose launcher sets it — only scripts/dr_benchmark/run_gate_b.py and wave2_stage_*.py do). So the function returns ("", 0, 0) BEFORE the LLM call and BEFORE `_apply_synthesis_deviation_screen` at :785 ever runs.

4) RUNTIME PROOF. Across all six real compose gear runs (/workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter{1,1_pinned,2,2_newest,3,4}/compose.log, ~16,500 lines incl. the 10,721-line iter-4 run): `grep -ic analyst` = 0 everywhere except ONE line in iter2_newest, which is generated PROSE containing "loan application analysts" — not the module. `grep -ic deviation` = 0 in all six. Even hypothetically, it is a sequential `await` inside one coroutine at report finalization, AFTER section rendering — no per-basket gathers are in flight for it to freeze. "Stalls concurrent regen/M-50 gathers" is asserted, not shown, and contradicted by the sequencing at :10986.

The bug CLASS is real (sync ThreadPoolExecutor + fut.result() from an async def is a genuine event-loop block, worth an asyncio.to_thread if the report path is ever enabled with PROMOTE=1). As a throughput killer for compose today it contributes exactly zero.
result.real_impact: Zero on the compose gear runs: the path is not executed (0 log hits across ~16.5k lines of 6 real runs; run_s5_i3.py never calls generate_multi_section_report). Absolute worst case if someone enabled it (PG_SWEEP_ANALYST_SYNTHESIS=1 + PG_ANALYST_SYNTHESIS_PROMOTE_GROUNDED=1 + research_plan=None): a ONE-SHOT event-loop block hard-bounded by the TOTAL wall deadline `_deadline_s()` = PG_ANALYST_SYNTHESIS_DEVIATION_DEADLINE_S, default 60.0s (analyst_synthesis_deviation_check.py:67), with pool.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True) so it cannot exceed it. <=60s once at finalization on a multi-hour 346-basket run = under 1%, stalling nothing concurrent. The fix is a 1-line hygiene change (await asyncio.to_thread at analyst_synthesis.py:785), not a throughput win.

result.notes:
PRIMARY ARTIFACT: /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log (1.5MB, 10631 lines, run STILL LIVE at time of writing: started 08:33:12, elapsed 385 min, PID 966951). This is the real 346-basket compose run. It has produced ZERO cp5 in 6h25m across 5 attempts.
