SELF-CONTENTION (operator asked): CONFIRMED. Two root-owned compose jobs overlapped on the SAME OpenRouter account for 2h11m — `launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh` (PID 817298, 08:33 -> now) and `run_sonnet_hands_compose.sh` (12:09:51, relaunched 12:55:55, ran to 14:20:01). Note the one that FINISHED (sonnet_hands, cp5 written, EXIT=0, 84 min) is the one that used `--cap-primary 8`; iter4 uses `--cap-primary 0` and is mathematically unable to finish (see finding 4).

ERROR/RETRY TALLY over the run: 3693 outbound POSTs (3681x200, 12x429 at the httpx layer), 762 WARNINGs, of which 562 are `openrouter_client "Connection error, retrying in 2.0s/4.0s"`, 61 total 429 mentions, 23x503, 21x502, 18x500, 26 writer transport-disconnects, 7 `asyncio timeout after 630s`. Sustained achieved throughput during attempt 4 = 10.7 POST/min = 0.18 POST/sec, against a configured cap of PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=30 / writer 24.

Files: /workspace/compose_wt/scripts/run_s5_i3.py, /workspace/compose_wt/src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py, /workspace/compose_wt/src/polaris_graph/generator/verified_compose.py, /workspace/compose_wt/src/providers/llm_provider.py, /workspace/gear_sync.sh, /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log

findings.title: The 0615bc5 missing-await bug EXISTS AGAIN at a hotter call site: _compose_section_per_basket is a sync def called with no await/to_thread from the async _run_section — it blocks the event loop for the ENTIRE per-basket compose of a section
findings.mechanism: `_compose_section_per_basket` is defined as a plain `def` (verified_compose.py:3262), not `async def`. `_run_section` (multi_section_generator.py:5182) is `async def`. At :5468 it is called as `_vc_composed = _compose_section_per_basket(...)` — no `await`, no `asyncio.to_thread`. This function is the MAIN per-basket compose loop: it walks every basket in the section, runs writer_fn + verify_fn + the bounded repair loop (redraft_fn) for each. All of it executes synchronously ON the asyncio event loop. Consequence: while section A is inside it, the single event loop is pinned — section B's coroutine cannot resume, and no in-flight writer/judge HTTP response can be processed. PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2 is therefore fiction: the two 'parallel' sections are serialized. The same file already uses `await asyncio.to_thread(strict_verify, ...)` at :5667 and :10288, so the authors knew the pattern — :5468 and :5514 were simply missed, exactly as _draft_passes_wrapper was.
findings.evidence: grep confirms `def _compose_section_per_basket(` at verified_compose.py:3262 (sync) and `async def _run_section(` at multi_section_generator.py:5182; the only two call sites (:5468, :5514) have no await/to_thread. MEASURED consequence in the live log: sections 0 and 1 both START at 12:04:58 (attempt 4) and report `[section 0] DONE 2945s` / `[section 1] DONE 2787s` — near-identical ~2900s walls rather than overlapping. Same in attempt 2 (3266s / 3036s) and attempt 5 (2843s / 3003s). Two genuinely-parallel sections of 18 and 15 baskets should not each cost ~2900s; the sum-shaped walls are the signature of serialization. Sustained POST rate during attempt 4 measured at 10.7 POST/min = 0.18 POST/sec against a configured cap of 30 concurrent.
findings.fix_sketch: Wrap both call sites: `_vc_composed = await asyncio.to_thread(functools.partial(_compose_section_per_basket, _vc_baskets, evidence_pool, writer_fn=..., verify_fn=..., redraft_fn=..., ...))`. This is the identical remedy applied in 0615bc5. Note it must be done TOGETHER with the redraft fix below, otherwise the offloaded thread still spins a fresh loop per redraft.

findings.title: _redraft spins a FRESH asyncio event loop per call, which makes get_semaphore() destroy and recreate the module-global LLM concurrency semaphore — 303 times in one run. PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM is not enforced at all.
findings.mechanism: `_redraft` (the group re-draft closure fed into _compose_one_basket's bounded repair loop) is a sync function. At :4993-4994 it creates a new `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)` and does `fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run()))` — a BRAND NEW event loop for every single re-draft. Inside `_run()`, `_call_writer` -> `OpenRouterClient._call_api` (openrouter_client.py:1890) calls `get_semaphore()`. get_semaphore (llm_provider.py:149) guards on `_LLM_SEMAPHORE_LOOP is not running_loop` and, on mismatch, REBINDS the module-global `_LLM_SEMAPHORE = asyncio.Semaphore(cap)`. Because the redraft loop is never the main compose loop, every alternation main-loop -> redraft-loop -> main-loop destroys the semaphore TWICE. Permits held by calls already in flight on the old object simply vanish; the fresh semaphore starts at full cap. So the global cap of 30 bounds nothing, and burst concurrency is unbounded — which is what produces the 429/connection-error storm. Separately, `fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0)` at :4996 is a BLOCKING wait of up to 330s, and because of finding 1 it executes on the event loop.
findings.evidence: MEASURED in the live log: `grep -c 'semaphore (re)initialized' compose.log` = 303, every one of them `max=30`. PROVED with a probe reproducing the exact pattern (fresh ThreadPoolExecutor + asyncio.run + get_semaphore): main-loop sem id 140225449962576 -> redraft-loop sem id 140225450057808 -> main-loop sem id AGAIN 140225449967120; `a is b` -> False. Probe also showed permit loss: after 2 acquires on the original semaphore its value was 28; after one redraft the newly-created semaphore reports value 30 — the 2 in-flight permits are gone, i.e. the cap is silently exceeded. Downstream in the real log: 562 `Connection error, retrying in 2.0s/4.0s` warnings, 61 429 mentions, 23x503, 21x502, 18x500.
findings.fix_sketch: Make _redraft async (`async def _redraft`) and await `_call_writer` directly on the caller's loop — the ThreadPoolExecutor+asyncio.run sandwich exists only because the caller chain is sync. If it must stay sync, pass the running loop in and use `asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe(_run(), main_loop)` instead of `asyncio.run(_run())`. Independently, harden llm_provider.get_semaphore: key the semaphore in a `WeakKeyDictionary[loop -> Semaphore]` instead of clobbering one module global, so a second loop cannot destroy the first loop's cap.

findings.title: The SHA-gated section RESUME never fires: a root daemon (gear_sync.sh) overwrites the compose's PINNED cp3 every ~96 min, so every relaunch declares all finished sections stale and recomposes them. Resume hit rate = 0/5 attempts.
findings.mechanism: run_s5_i3.py:506-523 hashes cp2/cp3/cp4, compares to ckpt/inputs_sha.json, and on ANY mismatch sets `_stale=True` -> skips loading every `section_*_draft.json` -> recomposes all 5 sections from scratch. Meanwhile /workspace/gear_sync.sh runs an infinite `while true; sleep 20` loop as root (PID 575438, up since Jul 10 22:08) whose `mirror` function does `cp -f $src $SH/s3_gear/cp3_basket_snapshot.json` whenever the S2/S3 gear emits a newer basket snapshot. That destination file is EXACTLY the path both launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh and iter5 pin as CP3. So the compose's 'pinned' input mutates underneath the running compose. Mean interval between cp3 promotions = 96 min; a single compose attempt needs 3h+. Input MTBF << attempt duration, so the resume is guaranteed to be invalidated before it can ever be used. This is a livelock, not a slowdown: forward progress is structurally zero.
findings.evidence: `grep -c '\[resume\] loaded' compose.log` = 0 across all 5 attempts — the resume has NEVER skipped a section. Two explicit `[resume] input SHAs changed since checkpoint -> IGNORING stale section drafts, recomposing all sections` at log lines 6941 (attempt 4) and 8503 (attempt 5). The input is visibly mutating mid-run: the `[load]` line reports cp3_baskets=343 (attempt 1), 343 (attempt 2), 346 (attempt 4), 329 (attempt 5) — three different basket counts inside ONE compose run. /workspace/gear_sync.log records 6 `PROMOTED s3_gear/cp3_basket_snapshot.json` events, 3 of them inside the iter4 window (09:46:44, 11:34:04, 12:53:18). WASTED WORK quantified from `draft kept basket=` events: 474 basket renders for only 341 distinct baskets = 133 duplicate renders (28% of all render work is redo); histogram of renders-per-basket: 284 baskets x1, 7 x2, 26 x3, 23 x4, 1 x6 — i.e. 57 baskets were rendered 3-6 times.
findings.fix_sketch: Two independent fixes, both needed. (a) The launcher must COPY cp2/cp3/cp4 into the run's own output dir at attempt-1 start and pass the copies, so a running compose has immutable inputs (snapshot-on-start). The 'gear rule: newest inputs win' can still apply BETWEEN runs, not DURING one. (b) run_s5_i3.py should key checkpoints per-input-SHA (e.g. ckpt/<sha12>/section_N_draft.json) so a changed input starts a new checkpoint namespace instead of silently discarding a valid one, and old namespaces remain resumable if the input reverts.

findings.title: 94% of all baskets (326 of 346) are dumped into a single residual section, and sections run under Semaphore(2) — so the run's makespan is one serial section that needs 11-23h. cp5 is unreachable at --cap-primary 0.
findings.mechanism: With `--cap-primary 0` (the DEFAULT, and what the gear launchers pass for a 'full-coverage acceptance run'), the section/basket map assigns 15-18 baskets each to sections 0-3 and sweeps everything else into a residual SectionPlan idx=4 — 326 of the 346 baskets. Sections are composed under `sec_sema = asyncio.Semaphore(PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS)` = 2 (run_s5_i3.py:491), and each section is one coroutine, so section 4 is a single indivisible serial unit. There is no intra-section fan-out across sections. The makespan of the whole compose is therefore set by section 4 alone, and cp5 is only written after ALL sections return. Compose parallelism is capped at 2 while 94% of the work sits in 1 unit — the 48-way parallelism the operator asked about cannot exist by construction.
findings.evidence: Log line 17/787: `[map] residual SectionPlan appended idx=4 ev_ids=326`; line 2519: `[section 4] START title='Additional Corroborated Findings' ev_ids=326`. Measured per-basket wall from the 9 completed section-DONE lines for sections 0-3: min 125s, mean 182s, max 252s per basket. Projected for 326 baskets: 11.3h (best) / 16.5h (mean) / 22.8h (worst). Observed reality: section 4 ran 10:29:06 -> 12:04:47 = 95 min and did NOT finish before the kill; it has never completed in any of 5 attempts. Longest surviving attempt = 2h57m. CONTROL: the one run that DID emit a cp5 (/workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_sonnet_hands, EXIT=0) used `--cap-primary 8`, which shrank the residual to `ev_ids=12` (`[section 4] DONE 2215s`), and the whole 5-section run finished in 84 min.
findings.fix_sketch: Shard the residual section: split its 326 baskets into ceil(326/K) sub-sections of ~16 baskets each and let them run under the same section semaphore (raise PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS accordingly), then concatenate. Basket rendering inside a section is already independent, so this is a pure fan-out. Alternatively, checkpoint at BASKET granularity inside a section rather than only at section completion, so section 4's 95 min of work is not thrown away on every kill.

findings.title: The entailment judge builds a brand-new ThreadPoolExecutor + OS thread for EVERY judge POST, and the OpenRouter client is re-instantiated 1405 times for 3693 POSTs
findings.mechanism: `_post_with_total_deadline` (entailment_judge.py:139) does `ex = concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)` then `fut = ex.submit(client.post, ...)` then `ex.shutdown()` — one executor + one OS thread created and torn down per NLI judge POST, purely to get a `fut.result(timeout=total_s)` wall. Every rendered sentence is judged, so this is per-sentence thread churn. Separately, `OpenRouter client initialized` appears 1405 times for 3693 POSTs, i.e. a client (and its httpx connection pool) is rebuilt roughly every 2.6 requests — connection pooling and TLS session reuse are largely defeated, which is the most likely proximate cause of the 562 `Connection error, retrying` warnings rather than genuine network faults.
findings.evidence: Read entailment_judge.py:156-180 — the executor is constructed inside the per-POST function, not module-level. From the live log: `grep -c 'OpenRouter client initialized'` = 1405 vs `grep -c 'HTTP Request: POST https://openrouter.ai'` = 3693. `grep -c 'Connection error, retrying'` = 562 (445 at 2.0s backoff + 117 at 4.0s), against only 12 real 429s at the httpx layer — the retry volume is ~46x the rate-limit volume, which points at transport/pool churn, not the provider.
findings.fix_sketch: Hoist a single module-level ThreadPoolExecutor (or use `httpx` async + `asyncio.wait_for`, which needs no thread at all) instead of one executor per POST. Hoist the OpenRouterClient to one long-lived instance per process (or per section) so the httpx connection pool and TLS sessions are actually reused across the 3693 POSTs.

result.lane: DO THE CONCURRENCY KNOBS ACTUALLY BIND? — trace PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY / PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM / PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY to the semaphore that uses them; find the smallest effective cap.
result.notes:
SMALLEST EFFECTIVE CAP IN THE CHAIN = 1, then 4. The chain on the compose path (post-0615bc5), per section:

  sections=2 (PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS, run_s5_i3.py:491 — LIVE)
    -> pre-pass writer=12/section (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY, abstractive_writer.py:986 — LIVE)
       -> global LLM=16 (PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM, openrouter_client.py:1890 — LIVE for writer, undercuts 2x12=24; LEAKY, see F3)
       -> NLI judge=4 (PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY default, entailment_judge.py:155 — LIVE, BINDING, never set by the launcher)
    -> authoritative compose loop = 1 (multi_section_generator.py:5468 — sync def on the event loop)

0615bc5 unblocked the PRE-PASS. It did not touch the compose loop, which is the phase that dominates section wall time (section 0 of the iter4 run: 591s pre-pass out of 3266s total => 82% of the section ran at concurrency 1).

RECOMMENDED ORDER: (1) `await asyncio.to_thread(_compose_section_per_basket, ...)` at :5468/:5514 — but note _redraft's asyncio.run then needs a re-look; (2) export PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=16..24 in launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh (it is the tightest live cap once the loop is unblocked, and it is a threading.BoundedSemaphore so raising it is safe across threads); (3) fix the per-loop rebind of the global LLM semaphore, or accept that PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM is decorative.

Probes: /tmp/probe_judge_cap.py (judge cap, measured), /tmp/ov.py (log overlap analyzer). Log mined: /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log (real 346-basket compose run, 6.4h).

findings.title: The AUTHORITATIVE compose loop is a sync def called with no await from an async coroutine — the whole per-basket verify+redraft phase runs ON the event loop, concurrency 1. 0615bc5 fixed the pre-pass; this is the same bug one layer up and it is still at HEAD.
findings.mechanism: `async def _run_section` (:5182) awaits `abstractive_pre_pass` (:5448 — now correctly parallel) and then calls `_vc_composed = _compose_section_per_basket(...)` at :5468 with NO await and NO asyncio.to_thread. `_compose_section_per_basket` is a plain `def` (verified_compose.py:3262). Inside it, per basket: (a) `verify_fn(sentence, pool)` = verify_sentence_provenance -> _get_judge().judge() (provenance_generator.py:2558) -> _post_with_total_deadline -> `fut.result(timeout=...)`, which BLOCKS THE CALLING THREAD; (b) `redraft_fn(...)` (verified_compose.py:2136) = the SYNTH_PRIMARY group re-draft, whose body does `fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run())); fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0)` (multi_section_generator.py:4994-4996) — a blocking wait of up to 330s on a FULL writer LLM call. The calling thread is the asyncio event loop. So for the entire compose phase of a section, the loop is frozen: no other section's pre-pass tasks run, no other basket's writer call runs, and even the judge's own 4-slot semaphore is irrelevant because only one judge call is ever issued at a time. The same defect exists on the non-abstractive branch at :5514. Commit 0615bc5 touched ONLY abstractive_writer.py (`git show --name-only`), so this path is untouched.
findings.evidence:
MEASURED from the real 346-basket run /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log. Section 0's pre-pass completes at 08:43:04 ("pre-pass complete: 12/12 baskets drafted ... elapsed=591.1s"); section 0 then enters _compose_section_per_basket. Window 08:43:04-08:57:28 (864s):
  - writer generate() calls: 21, MAX OVERLAP = 1 (overlap histogram {1: 21} — every call ran ALONE)
  - 22 "[llm_provider] Concurrency semaphore (re)initialized" lines = 22 fresh event loops = these 21 calls are _redraft's asyncio.run workers, each blocking the loop on fut.result()
  - 131 OpenRouter POSTs / 14 min = 9.1 POST/min, median inter-POST gap 4.4s => strictly serial
  - section 1 (running CONCURRENTLY under sec_sema=2, 15 baskets, its pre-pass still in flight) drafted exactly 1 basket in those 864s ("draft kept basket" count = 1). Its pre-pass elapsed 1455.7s for 15 baskets vs section 0's 591.1s for 12 — 2.5x slower for 1.25x the work, because it was starved by section 0's compose loop.
Section totals: section 0 DONE 3266s of which the parallel pre-pass was 591s => 82% of the section ran at concurrency 1.
findings.fix_sketch: `_vc_composed = await asyncio.to_thread(_compose_section_per_basket, _vc_baskets, evidence_pool, writer_fn=..., verify_fn=..., ...)` at :5468 and :5514 (functools.partial for the kwargs). That frees the loop so sibling sections' pre-passes keep drafting, and lets the judge's own semaphore (see next finding) actually admit 4+ concurrent POSTs instead of 1. Note _redraft's inner `asyncio.run` (:4994) already runs on its own worker thread, so it stays correct off-loop.

findings.title: PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY is LIVE and BINDING on the compose path at its default of 4 — and the compose launcher never sets it. It is the smallest live semaphore in the chain.
findings.mechanism: `_post_with_total_deadline` (entailment_judge.py:139) wraps EVERY judge POST in `with _acquire_judge_slot():` at :155. `acquire_judge_slot` (judge_concurrency.py:151) acquires a PROCESS-GLOBAL `threading.BoundedSemaphore(bound)` built at judge_concurrency.py:145, with `bound = resolve_max_concurrency()` -> `os.environ.get('PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY', '')`; empty => DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4 (judge_concurrency.py:55). This is the ONLY POST path in the judge (called from judge() at :999). The compose path reaches it via writer_verify_fn -> verify_sentence_provenance -> _get_judge().judge() (provenance_generator.py:2558/:2688), both in the pre-pass (abstractive_writer.py:833/:912, now correctly in asyncio.to_thread) and in the compose loop. `launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh` exports PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 and PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 but NEVER exports PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY (checked all 10 launch scripts: only scripts/dr_benchmark/run_gate_b.py:1695 sets it, to 2). So once 0615bc5 unblocked the loop, the 12 (or 24) parallel writer verify threads all pile onto a 4-wide gate. The semaphore is also SHARED with credibility_judge_caller and semantic_conflict_detector, so any concurrent advisory pass steals from the same 4.
findings.evidence:
MEASURED with /tmp/probe_judge_cap.py — patches only _EntailmentJudge._build_client to a fake whose .post() sleeps 1.0s, leaves PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY unset (exactly the compose launcher env), fires 24 concurrent judge() calls from 24 threads:
  resolve_max_concurrency() = 4
  N=24 concurrent judge() calls (1.0s POST each) -> PEAK in-flight POSTs = 4, wall = 6.01s
  (unbounded would be 1.0s; cap-4 predicts 24/4 = 6s — matched to 0.01s)
So 24 writer-verify threads get exactly 4 concurrent NLI POSTs. Real NLI latency is 6-40s (per the _ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S=150 comment at entailment_judge.py:135), so at cap 4 the NLI stage tops out at roughly 4/20s = 12 POST/min — which is essentially the 9.1-9.6 POST/min measured end-to-end across BOTH real compose runs (s5_gear_iter4: 3699 POSTs / 384 min = 9.6/min; s5_sonnet_hands: 1183 / 130 min = 9.1/min).
findings.fix_sketch: export PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=16 (or 24) in launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh, matching the writer concurrency. It is a threading.BoundedSemaphore, so raising it is thread-safe; the judge already holds per-thread httpx clients (threading.local, entailment_judge.py:680) and the verdict cache is RLock-guarded. Raise it in step with PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY, and watch for the 429s it was originally added to suppress (judge_concurrency.py:4-24 documents the 32-way burst that stormed friendli — but 32 was unbounded, 16 is not).

findings.title: PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM is neither global nor stable: it does not cover the NLI judge at all (~65% of all OpenRouter POSTs), and its semaphore is torn down and rebuilt on every event-loop switch — 306 times in a single run.
findings.mechanism: Two independent leaks. (1) NOT GLOBAL: the semaphore is only acquired in `OpenRouterClient._call` (openrouter_client.py:1889-1890). The entailment judge does NOT use OpenRouterClient — it builds its own httpx.Client (entailment_judge.py:712 `_build_client`) and POSTs directly to `self._endpoint`. So every NLI judge call — the majority of the traffic — bypasses PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM entirely. In the iter4 run: 3699 total OpenRouter POSTs, of which only 1258 are writer generate() calls => ~2441 (66%) are judge POSTs, completely outside the 'global' cap. (2) NOT STABLE: get_semaphore() at :149 does `if _LLM_SEMAPHORE is None or _LLM_SEMAPHORE_LOOP is not running_loop: _LLM_SEMAPHORE = asyncio.Semaphore(cap)` — it REPLACES the module-global whenever the running loop differs. `_redraft` (multi_section_generator.py:4994) submits `asyncio.run(_run())` to a worker thread, creating a BRAND NEW event loop per re-draft. Each redraft therefore forces a rebuild in the worker loop, and the main loop's next writer call forces a rebuild back — ping-pong. Every rebuild discards the in-flight count: tasks still holding the OLD semaphore object are invisible to the NEW one, which hands out `cap` fresh slots. The cap is not enforced across the alternation, which feeds the 429s the run then burns 15-50s backoffs on.
findings.evidence: grep 'Concurrency semaphore (re)initialized' /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log -c => 306 rebuilds in one run. In the 864s section-0 compose window alone: 22 rebuilds for 21 generate() calls — i.e. essentially one new event loop per writer call, exactly matching the _redraft asyncio.run pattern. grep for get_semaphore across src/ returns exactly ONE non-provider caller: openrouter_client.py:1890 (the writer/section LLM path). entailment_judge.py has no reference to it — it goes through judge_concurrency's separate threading.BoundedSemaphore instead. The 12 observed '429 Too Many Requests' judge warnings in the same log are consistent with an unenforced total-QPS cap.
findings.fix_sketch: Either (a) make the cap a threading-level bound shared by both transports (the judge's own bounded semaphore is already the right primitive — unify them), or (b) at minimum stop the per-loop rebind churn: cache the semaphore per-loop in a WeakKeyDictionary keyed by loop instead of replacing the single global. And stop creating a fresh event loop per re-draft — _redraft should reuse a persistent worker loop or be made async and awaited from an off-loop thread.

findings.title: PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 is LIVE but is silently undercut by PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 whenever two sections overlap (2 x 12 = 24 > 16), and is the ONLY lever on the 288-basket residual section that holds 83% of the corpus.
findings.mechanism: `sem = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)` at :986 with concurrency read at :955 from PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY — genuinely LIVE, one basket task per basket, `async with semaphore` at :993, and `asyncio.wait(tasks, timeout=wall)` at :1055 (not a gather-of-1). But the semaphore is created PER abstractive_pre_pass CALL, i.e. per section, and run_s5_i3.py:491 runs 2 sections at once (`sec_sema = asyncio.Semaphore(int(os.environ.get('PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS', '2')))`, launcher exports 2). So up to 24 writer tasks contend for the 16-slot global LLM semaphore — the global cap wins, and the per-stage knob's headroom is dead. Worse, the basket distribution is pathological: the iter4 log shows sections of 18/15/15/17 ev_ids plus a residual section with 326 ev_ids (the writer logs 'basket-scaled wall: n=288 conc=24'). For most of the run only the residual is live, so PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS gives zero parallelism on 83% of the work and writer conc 12 is the entire budget for 288 baskets => 24 sequential waves, each gated on the 4-wide NLI judge.
findings.evidence: Measured writer-call overlap across the whole iter4 run (which used conc=24 / global=30): MAX overlap 29, mean concurrency at call start 12.89 — so the writer semaphore does admit parallel calls, but the histogram shows 243 of 1258 calls started completely alone (concurrency 1), the compose-loop-window calls. Log lines: '[abstractive_writer] basket-scaled wall: n=288 conc=24' and '[section 4] START title=Additional Corroborated Findings ev_ids=326' vs sections 0-3 at 15-18 ev_ids. Launcher: launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh:34-35,43 (writer 12, global 16, sections 2).
findings.fix_sketch: Raise PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM to >= sections x writer_conc (2 x 12 = 24, or just set it to 32) so the per-stage knob is the binding one, and split the residual section into several so PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS has something to parallelize. Confirm the split by watching for >1 concurrent '[section N] START' with N>=4.

findings.title: Two different env names for the section-parallelism knob; the one documented in multi_section_generator is INERT on the compose path.
findings.mechanism: multi_section_generator's `generate_multi_section` reads `PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS` (:9927) into its own `sem = asyncio.Semaphore(_section_concurrency)` (:9938), with a hardcoded default of max_parallel_sections=3. But the compose driver scripts/run_s5_i3.py does NOT call generate_multi_section — it builds SectionPlans itself and calls `_run_section` directly under its OWN semaphore, reading a DIFFERENT name: `PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS` (run_s5_i3.py:491, default 2). So PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS (and generate_multi_section's semaphore, and _gather_sections_isolated at :980) are dead code on the compose path. An operator who tunes PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS gets nothing. This is the exact 'knobs in this repo lie' pattern the brief warned about.
findings.evidence: grep -rn 'PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS|PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS': the 10 launch_*.sh scripts ALL export PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS (=2, iter3 used 3); NOTHING exports PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS. run_s5_i3.py has exactly one Semaphore (line 491) and one gather (line 597, over the per-section tasks) — it never touches generate_multi_section.
findings.fix_sketch: Make run_s5_i3.py:491 read both names (PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS or PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS) or delete the unused one, and log the resolved value at startup alongside the other caps so the effective chain is visible in compose.log.

findings.title: asyncio.to_thread's default executor (min(32, cpu+4) = 32 threads) is now a shared, undocumented cap on the off-loop verify path introduced by 0615bc5.
findings.mechanism: The two new `await asyncio.to_thread(_draft_passes_wrapper, ...)` calls (:833, :912) use the loop's DEFAULT ThreadPoolExecutor, which Python 3.11 sizes at min(32, os.cpu_count() + 4) = 32 on this 128-core box. That pool is SHARED with `await asyncio.to_thread(strict_verify, ...)` (multi_section_generator.py:5667, :5804), the credibility-pass to_thread (:9557), and the rewrite pass (:10288). At the current PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 x 2 sections = 24 verify threads it does not bind — but it is only 8 threads of headroom, and raising the writer knob above ~28 would silently hit this ceiling instead of the knob's value. Each verify thread additionally holds its slot while _post_with_total_deadline spawns yet ANOTHER ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1) per POST (entailment_judge.py:156).
findings.evidence: nproc = 128 => min(32, 132) = 32 default executor workers (Python 3.11.10, confirmed `python3 -V`). grep to_thread across the generator: 4 distinct call sites all sharing the default executor. Not binding today (24 < 32), which is why I rank it LOW — but it is the next ceiling after PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY is raised.
findings.fix_sketch: Install an explicit, sized executor on the compose loop (`loop.set_default_executor(ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=writer_conc*sections + 16))`) so the writer knob, not an implicit stdlib default, is the bound.

result.reason:
Refuted on three independent grounds.

(1) PREMISE DEAD IN HEAD. The claim explicitly rests on "Reached from the compose leg (finding 1), that time.sleep executes on the asyncio event loop." Finding 1 is exactly what commit 0615bc5 fixed. In the current worktree, abstractive_writer.py:833 and :912 are both `await asyncio.to_thread(_draft_passes_wrapper, ...)`, and every verify site in contract_section_runner.py (:2076, :2093, :2109, :2133) is likewise `await asyncio.to_thread(...)`. judge() therefore runs on a worker thread, never the loop. Box has 128 CPUs, so the default to_thread executor is min(32, cpu+4) = 32 workers vs writer concurrency 12 (iter5) / 24 (iter3-4); no custom executor narrows it, so the pool is never starved. PROBE (/tmp/probe.py) reproducing the exact HEAD structure (async with sem -> to_thread -> sleeping sync judge, 12 baskets, one backing off) measured max event-loop stall of 50.8ms (just the heartbeat tick) and the 11 sibling baskets completed unaffected. The time.sleep stalls exactly ONE worker thread.

(2) THE SLEEP DOESN'T EVEN BLOCK OTHER JUDGES. entailment_judge.py:155 `with _acquire_judge_slot():` wraps ONLY the POST inside _post_with_total_deadline. The time.sleep at :1166/:1209 is in judge()'s retry loop, OUTSIDE that context manager. The docstring at :151 states this deliberately: "The slot is held only for the duration of THIS POST (not the caller's retry backoffs) and is released on every exit path." A backing-off judge holds zero judge slots — the system was already designed against the exact failure mode alleged.

(3) MAGNITUDE IS NOT HIGH, EVEN GRANTING THE CLAIM'S OWN WORST CASE. Grepped the full 122MB / 1485-file log corpus. Rate-limit backoffs appear in only 5 real compose logs. The largest (/workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log, 6h28m wall: 08:33:12 -> 15:01:17 = 23,285s, 10,732 lines) contains exactly 12 rate-limit backoffs, ALL at the 15.0s floor, never once escalating toward the 60s cap. Total = 180s. Pre-fix worst case (on-loop, fully serializing) = 180/23285 = 0.77% of wall. Post-fix (1 of 12 writer slots) = ~15s aggregate = 0.064% of wall. Even a hypothetical 10x-worse storm lands at ~0.6%.

BONUS KILL — the cited docstring is INVERTED. The claim asserts "The module's own docstring says 429 storms on the mirror-chain lead host are the expected steady state." It says the reverse: judge_concurrency.py:8-20 describes the friendli-lead-host 429 storm as a HISTORICAL failure that bounded concurrency FIXED ("Fewer simultaneous POSTs per host -> fewer 429s"), and entailment_judge.py:149 says the per-claim burst "can no longer 429-storm the mirror-chain lead host." launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh:10,33 further cut concurrency to reduce residual 429 self-contention. The 429 rate is actively managed, not an accepted steady state.

The investigator read the code but did not measure, did not check that finding 1's fix already invalidated their premise, did not notice the slot is released before the backoff, and misread the docstring as asserting the opposite of what it says.
result.real_impact: Essentially nil as a throughput cap. Measured on the real 6h28m gear_iter4 run: 12 rate-limit backoffs, all at the 15.0s floor (never the 60s cap), 180s total. Post-fix the sleep runs on a worker thread and holds no judge slot, so its only real cost is that a backing-off basket holds 1 of 12 writer-semaphore slots for 15s (abstractive_writer.py:992, `async with semaphore` spans all of _pre_pass_one_basket) => ~15s of aggregate lost throughput across 6.5 hours = 0.064% of wall. Even the claim's own worst-case (pre-fix, on-loop, fully serializing) is only 0.77% of wall. Not worth an engineering cycle; moreover the 15s floor is intentional — shortening it would re-trigger the retry-exhaustion fail-closed claim drops it was introduced to prevent (entailment_judge.py:206). Acting on this finding would be a dead end.

result.notes:
HEADLINE, stated plainly because the operator asked directly: CONTEXT SIZE IS NOT WHAT IS CAPPING COMPOSE. I tried hard to make the O(N)/O(N^2) hypothesis true and it is false. The writer prompt is strictly per-basket (abstractive_writer.py:566-576 loops only over THIS basket's SUPPORTS members) — no corpus digest, no outline body, no other baskets. I built all 328 real prompts from the actual cp2+cp3 snapshots: median 1,930 tok, p90 3,746, max 10,140, total 748k for a full pass. The iter4 log's own accounting agrees (median 2,889 in-tokens/call). Prompt size does NOT scale with corpus size. quantified_analysis.py is not even on the compose path (grep of run_s5_i3 + multi_section_generator = 0 hits).

THREE HYPOTHESES I KILLED WITH LIVE MEASUREMENTS (so nobody re-spends time on them):
1. "Big premise => slow TTFT" — FALSE. Live A/B on the production judge body (glm-5.2, reasoning=high, max_tokens=131072): same sentence with a 2,298-tok premise = 4.7s median vs a 66-tok premise = 4.8s median (3 reps each). GLM is DECODE-bound; 2k of prefill is free.
2. "max_tokens=131072 is throttling us" — FALSE. Live A/B at the production 12-way concurrency: max_tokens=131072 -> wall 4.8s, 0/12 429s; max_tokens=1024 -> wall 4.8s, 0/12 429s. Identical. The §9.1.8 always-MAX slate costs nothing. It is also not starving anything: measured writer reasoning tokens median 406, max 8,924 — nowhere near the 65,536 cap.
3. "We're missing prompt caching" — ALREADY WORKING, and it is a COST lever only, not throughput. No cache_control anywhere in src/, but GLM-5.2 on OpenRouter does implicit prefix caching and it engages: identical 1,430-tok prompt twice -> cached_tokens 192 then 1,408; cost $0.00058 -> $0.00018 (3.2x). Prompts are already structured correctly for it (judge: template->SPAN->SENTENCE; writer: system 445 + outline lead 651 + lead 79 = 1,175-tok stable prefix ahead of the variable spans; revise_reasons appended last). Latency was unchanged.

THE NUMBER THAT MATTERS AND POINTS OUT OF MY LANE: iter4 median writer-call latency = 46.3s (p90 180.4s, max 2,163.8s). The SAME call shape uncontended takes ~4.8s. That is a ~10x gap explained by NONE of the above — corr(input_tokens, latency) = -0.001 in iter4. It is queueing/contention. Related: 1,258 writer calls for ~328 baskets = 3.8 calls/basket (retry amplification), and the judge is 2/3 of all OpenRouter traffic (2,436 of 3,694 POSTs). All 59 429s in the iter4 log land on the judge. Recommend the concurrency/retry lane chase those.

Rate limits: OpenRouter 429s here come back through the judge and the code honors Retry-After with a 15s floor / 60s cap (entailment_judge.py:216-219). I could not reproduce a token-weighted 429 at 12-way concurrency, so I cannot claim token-based limiting is biting — the 429s look like request/concurrency pressure, plausibly from sibling worktree jobs on the shared account.

Probes left in /tmp: probe_prompt_size.py, probe_judge_premise.py, probe_judge_live.py, probe_cache_and_maxtok.py.

findings.title: PG_S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP=8000 is a DEAD env var — exported by all 9 launchers, read by ZERO lines of code; spans reach the writer unbounded and set the makespan tail
findings.mechanism: Every launcher exports PG_S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP=8000, so operators believe span text entering the writer prompt is capped at 8000 chars. Nothing reads it. `grep -rn S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP` over src/ + scripts/ + config/ in BOTH trees returns 0 hits; 9 .sh files set it. _build_writer_prompt (:574) appends `f"SPAN {i}: {quote}"` with no truncation anywhere in the writer path. So the member's full direct_quote — which run_s5_i3.build_baskets sets to the ENTIRE evidence row text — goes in whole. Measured on the real corpus: row text median 2,527 chars, p90 11,435, max 24,915. Resulting writer prompts reach 11,389 in-tokens (from iter4's own log accounting). THROUGHPUT MECHANISM: in the uncontended regime latency scales steeply with basket size. From the iter3 log, median latency bucketed by input tokens: <2k -> 4.3s; 2-4k -> 12.4s; 4-6k -> 28.4s; 6-8k -> 28.8s; 8-10k -> 70.2s; 10-12k -> 98.5s. A 23x spread. These fat baskets are the TAIL that sets the wave makespan: _makespan_wall_seconds and the 300s PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CALL_DEADLINE_S are both sized on averages, so a handful of 100-250s baskets stretches every wave and eats the wall. HONEST CAVEAT: in iter4 corr(IN,latency) = -0.001 because provider queueing swamped the size effect — this is a tail/makespan cost, not a mean-latency cost. The fix is one line: the cap the operator already thinks is running.
findings.evidence: grep: 9 launchers set PG_S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP, 0 readers in src/scripts/config across /home/polaris/wt/compose AND /workspace/POLARIS. Built all 328 real prompts from cp2_corpus_snapshot.json + cp3_basket_snapshot.json: median 1,930 tok, p90 3,746, max 10,140. iter3 compose.log latency-by-input-bucket (n=162 calls): 4.3s @<2k -> 98.5s @10-12k, corr(IN,latency)=+0.182. iter4 log: max writer prompt 11,389 in-tokens.
findings.fix_sketch: Actually wire the knob. In _build_writer_prompt (abstractive_writer.py:569), truncate the span at the cap the launchers already set: `cap = int(os.environ.get('PG_S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP', '8000') or 8000); quote = quote[:cap]`. NOTE: the provenance token [#ev:eid:start-end] is derived from _member_global_span independently, so truncating the DISPLAYED span without shrinking the token would desync the region gate — safest is to trim the member (the _member_surviving_units_or_drop envelope path at :740 already does exactly this kind of re-anchoring via dataclasses.replace + a re-derived span) so token and text stay consistent. Better still, cap at the sentence-unit level so the fat tail collapses without losing the claim-bearing text.

findings.title: NLI judge premise is the ENTIRE evidence row, not the claim-bearing sentences — a ~9x input-token overcharge on 2/3 of all OpenRouter traffic
findings.mechanism: `combined_span = " ".join(span_texts)` where each span_text comes from get_span_text (clinical_generator/provenance.py:162 -> `text[token.span_start:token.span_end]`), and the token's span is _member_global_span (verified_compose.py:307-325) = the offsets of the member's WHOLE direct_quote inside the row. Since build_baskets sets direct_quote = the full row text, the premise handed to the NLI judge is the entire multi-KB document, for every sentence, on every verification pass. An entailment judge needs the one or two sentences that carry the claim. MEASURED on the real snapshot (n=379 member-spans): premise median 1,134 tok, p90 2,645, p99 3,439, max 4,986; 53% exceed 1,000 tokens and 32% exceed 2,000. NLI needs ~66. iter4 fired 2,436 judge POSTs (3,694 total POSTs minus 1,258 writer `generate completed` lines), so ~3.84M input tokens went to the judge where ~0.4M would do. IMPORTANT AND HONEST: this does NOT buy latency — I A/B'd it live and a 2,298-tok premise (4.7s) is indistinguishable from a 66-tok premise (4.8s) because GLM-5.2 is decode-bound. So this is a COST + rate-limit-weight defect, not a latency defect. It matters for throughput only via 429 pressure: all 59 429s in the iter4 log land on the judge, and each triggers a 15s-floor backoff (entailment_judge.py:216-219). Also note the judge runs the SAME model as the writer (z-ai/glm-5.2, entailment_judge.py:106) at reasoning effort HIGH with max_tokens=131072 to emit ~30 tokens of JSON — the reasoning decode, not the premise, is what costs the ~4.8s.
findings.evidence: Live A/B, production judge body: FULL premise 2,298 tok -> 10.4/4.7/4.4s (median 4.7s); TIGHT premise 66 tok -> 13.2/4.8/2.2s (median 4.8s). No latency win. Premise size distribution measured over all 328 baskets: median 1,134 tok, max 4,986, 53% >1k. Judge POST count from iter4 compose.log: 3,694 total - 1,258 writer = 2,436. Cacheable stable prefix is only the 212-tok template = 13.5% of a median judge prompt.
findings.fix_sketch: Shrink the NLI premise to the claim-bearing sentence units rather than the whole row. The machinery already exists: _member_surviving_units_or_drop (abstractive_writer.py:740) already splits a member into sentence units and re-anchors a trimmed envelope via dataclasses.replace + a re-derived _member_global_span. Apply the same narrowing before the judge call so the premise is the local span, not the document. Expect ~9x fewer judge input tokens and materially less 429 weight; do NOT expect a latency win (measured). Independently: the judge would be far cheaper and faster with reasoning effort dropped from high on a task whose output is a 3-field JSON verdict — but that changes verdict semantics, so it needs a faithfulness A/B, not a unilateral flip.

result.reason: Mechanism exists but the impact claim is false by ~30x and mis-attributed. The code is real: multi_section_generator.py:4994 `fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s+30)` blocks the calling thread, and _compose_section_per_basket IS called sync-on-loop (multi_section_generator.py:5468, no await/to_thread), so redrafts do block the loop. The path is live. BUT I found a real 12-basket section run with PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1 (/tmp/w.log; pre-pass completes 08:43:04, compose stage 08:43:04->08:57:26 = 862s) and measured it: 21 post-pre-pass GLM-5.2 writer calls (these can only be the redraft — build_short_member_sentence at verified_compose.py:1056 is an explicitly deterministic NO-LLM writer, and the entailment judge uses a raw httpx.Client that never logs 'OpenRouter client initialized'). Their durations: total 121.6s, median 4.2s, MAX 14.4s. Zero 'SYNTH_PRIMARY group re-draft failed/timed-out' warnings => 0/21 approached the 300s deadline, let alone the 330s wall. Prompts are small (1.3k-8.5k input tokens), so a redraft is a ~4s call, not a 330s one. The claimed 330s is simply the timeout constant, not an observed cost. Worse for the claim: in that SAME serialized stage there were 131 POSTs total, i.e. ~110 NLI judge POSTs consuming ~740s of the 862s — the judge outblocks the redraft 6:1 inside the identical call frame. So the redraft is not "the" serializer; the whole synchronous _compose_section_per_basket frame is, and killing the redraft's fut.result() would un-serialize nothing (the sibling sync verify_fn calls keep the loop pinned). CRITICAL_serializes is not supportable.
result.real_impact: Survives only as a minor cost, not a cap. Measured: fires ~1.75x/basket (21 calls / 12 baskets, cap PG_WRITER_REPAIR_MAX=2), blocking the event loop 121.6s per 12 baskets = ~10.1s/basket, median 4.2s/call, max 14.4s/call, 0 timeouts. That is 14% of the 862s compose stage; the NLI judge POSTs in the same sync frame are ~740s (86%). Extrapolated to 346 baskets: ~3,500s (~58 min) of loop-block from redrafts vs ~5.9 hours from judge calls on the same on-loop path. Fixing this line alone buys at most ~14% of compose-stage wall and does NOT restore concurrency; the correct target is that _compose_section_per_basket (and its verify_fn NLI calls) runs synchronously on the event loop at multi_section_generator.py:5468.

result.reason:
Could not refute — the claim is confirmed by code AND by independent evidence from a real post-fix production run.

CODE: `_run_section` is `async def` (src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5182), awaited under a Semaphore in scripts/run_s5_i3.py:491/554/557/597. Both `_compose_section_per_basket(...)` call sites (:5468 abstractive branch, :5514 deterministic branch) are bare sync calls — `to_thread` in that file appears ONLY at :5667, :5804, :9557, :10288, none of which cover this leg. The callee is sync (`verified_compose.py:3262`), loops all baskets, and its verify_fn reaches provenance_generator.py `_get_judge().judge()` (entailment gate default `enforce`) -> entailment_judge.py `_post_with_total_deadline` -> `fut.result(timeout=...)` which blocks the calling thread = the event loop.

REACHED AT RUNTIME: launch_compose_gear_iter4.sh:19 sets PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER=1, so the :5468 branch runs; its downstream log line at :5604 ("verified-compose PRIMARY: N baskets") appears repeatedly in /workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log.

REFUTATIONS I TRIED AND THEY FAILED:
- Judge verdict cache (judge_verdict_cache.py, default ON) does NOT make the compose leg free: 220 live POSTs still occur in one compose window.
- Judge slot semaphore is NOT the real serializer: judge_concurrency.py:55 DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4 (env unset in the launch script), and the pre-pass phase achieves real overlap under that same cap.
- Not already guarded: no wallclock/to_thread wrapper covers the call.

MEASURED (POST-completion inter-arrival gaps from the real iter4 log, same process/run/account):
  pre-pass, both sections:            96 POSTs / 414s, 53.7% of gaps <0.5s, median gap 0.11s  -> genuinely concurrent
  sec1 COMPOSE while sec0 pre-passes: 120 POSTs / 648s,  1.7% of gaps <0.5s, median gap 4.24s -> sibling starved
  COMPOSE only (both sections):       220 POSTs /1883s,  0/219 gaps <0.5s,  median gap 5.27s -> achieved concurrency EXACTLY 1
Zero overlapping POSTs out of 219 during compose is statistically impossible for two independent in-flight streams; it is the exact signature of a single-threaded issuer, i.e. a blocked event loop.
result.real_impact:
Measured on the real 5-section run (/workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log):

1) The compose leg runs at achieved concurrency 1.0 despite PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2 and a judge cap of 4. Compose window: 220 POSTs over 1883s (7.0 POST/min) vs 13.9 POST/min in the pre-pass. The 2-way section parallelism collapses to 1 => the compose leg costs ~2x its necessary wall.

2) Compose is the MAJORITY of section wall, not a tail: section 0 DONE 3266s of which pre-pass was 1107.8s => ~2100s (65%) in the serialized compose leg. Section 1: 3036s total, 428.8s pre-pass.

3) Cross-section starvation is real and already causing DATA LOSS: while one section composes, the sibling's 24-way pre-pass collapses to serial (overlap 53.7% -> 1.7%). The co-running pre-pass took 1107.8s vs 428.8s unblocked, and one pre-pass hit the wall: compose.log:2606 "pre-pass WALL-DEADLINE 1700s hit: ABANDONING 3/14 still-pending basket task(s) -> K-span fallback (fail-open, disclosed)". So the on-loop block is not only slow, it is degrading 3 baskets to the fallback writer.

4) At 346-basket scale it dominates: "[map] primary_per_section={0: 15, 1: 12, 2: 14, 3: 14, 4: 288}" — 288 of the 346 baskets live in section 4 and every one of them is re-verified through this single serialized sync leg.

Expected gain from wrapping :5468 and :5514 in `await asyncio.to_thread(...)`: restores >=2x on the compose leg (true section parallelism, and each section's per-basket judge POSTs stop blocking the loop) AND restores the 24-way pre-pass concurrency for any section co-running with a composing sibling, which should also eliminate the 1700s pre-pass wall-deadline abandonments. On the observed windows that is roughly 2100s -> ~1000s per composing section pair, plus ~680s recovered on the starved pre-pass.

result.reason:
REFUTED on three independent grounds.

(1) The load-bearing mechanism is false. The claim asserts "the section is the unit of parallelism ... 94% of the work has an achieved parallelism of exactly 1." The code says otherwise: abstractive_writer.py:955 sets concurrency = PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY (12 iter5 / 24 iter3-4) and :986 `sem = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)` fans the section's baskets out concurrently INSIDE a single section (plus rec_sem at :1085 for the recovery pass). Section 4's 304 baskets run 12-way, not 1-way. The investigator even quotes launch_compose_gear_iter3.sh:2 ("section 4 = 276 baskets @ concurrency 4 -> ~4h makespan") as supporting evidence, but that comment is describing INTRA-section concurrency and therefore contradicts them.

(2) The measured "parallelism 1" IS finding #1, already fixed. Pre-0615bc5, _draft_passes_wrapper's blocking fut.result() froze the SHARED event loop, so sections 0 and 1 — which the log shows running concurrently under Semaphore(2) — were serialized against EACH OTHER too. The serialization was global, not sectional. Their 143s/basket serial-tail number is the missing await, not the scheduling order. The fix (await asyncio.to_thread at abstractive_writer.py:833 and :912) is at worktree HEAD.

(3) The late start costs ~zero makespan, simulated. The global cap PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 (providers/llm_provider.py:79, get_semaphore() :124) is shared across ALL nodes and is the binding constraint. During the small-section phase, demand is already 2 sections x writer 12 = 24 > 16 available — the box is oversubscribed. Starting section 4 at t=0 would only steal slots from sections 0-3. Discrete-event sim (/tmp/sim.py, real counts 18/15/15/17/304, writer=12, global=16): makespan = 600s at PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS = 2, 3, AND 5 — identical. Only sec4_start moves (80s -> 60s -> 0s); sec4_done never moves. The claim itself concedes "PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS cannot help," which makes it non-actionable by its own admission on top of being wrong about the mechanism.

The sec_sema at run_s5_i3.py:491 and the plan-order task creation at :588-597 are real code that is really reached — the ordering claim is factually accurate as a description of when section 4 starts. But ordering is not throughput. It reorders idle time that does not exist.
result.real_impact:
Zero throughput cost from the claimed mechanism. Makespan is identical at PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS = 2, 3, or 5 (600s in sim, all three), because the global LLM semaphore (16) is already saturated by 2 concurrent sections x 12 writer slots = 24 demand. Section 4's 90-minute "late start" is displaced idle time, not lost work: sections 0-3 are consuming the same 16 global slots during that window. The only residual effect is a sub-wave underfill at section transitions when a section has fewer than 12 baskets left — a few minutes on a multi-hour run, not CRITICAL_serializes.

ADJACENT REAL FINDING (not the claim, hand back separately): PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 < PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 (launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh:34-35). Once only section 4 remains — the entire 94%-of-work tail — the run can keep only 12 calls in flight against an allowed 16, idling 4 global slots for hours. Same sim with writer=16: makespan 600s -> 480s, a 20% tail win from a one-line env change. That is the actual lever in this neighborhood, and it has nothing to do with task-creation order or the section semaphore.

result.reason: CONFIRMED, could not kill it. (1) Code at HEAD: multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514 call _compose_section_per_basket(...) as a plain sync call from `async def _run_section` (:5182) — no await, no to_thread. Sections DO run concurrently (Semaphore at :9937 + asyncio.gather at :1005), so a blocked loop thread blocks all siblings. The codebase admits it: _make_group_redraft_fn's docstring (:4934) says "_compose_section_per_basket runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop, so the async writer call is executed in an ISOLATED worker-thread event loop (its own asyncio.run) and joined" — the join is fut.result(timeout=...) at :4997, ON THE EVENT-LOOP THREAD. (2) Path is reached: 6x "[activation] synth_primary: authored_prose kept=" logs from inside that loop during the window. (3) Verdict cache (entailment_judge.py:756) does not save it, because the dominant blocking call is not the judge. (4) NEW, HARDER PROOF than the investigator had: section 3's pre-pass started 13:58:56 with a 1700s wall, so the asyncio timer should have expired 14:27:16 — it actually fired at 14:36:28 (log:10128), 552 SECONDS LATE. (4) CONTEXT SIZE IS NOT THE CAP. Probed judge latency across real corpus spans: 628 -> 5,184 prompt tokens moved median latency 4.4s -> 4.8s. Flat. Prefill is cheap on this model; "the context is too large" is not the throughput story.

The 'GLM-5.2 reasoning truncation' failure documented on the outline wheel does NOT reproduce on the compose wheel: 0 occurrences in real logs, 0 in live probes.

WHAT I FOUND INSTEAD: chasing the judge call down to the transport surfaced the real remaining serializer — a process-global BoundedSemaphore(4) that every NLI verdict must pass through. This is the exact complement to commit 0615bc5: that fix moved the blocking judge POST OFF the event loop into threads via asyncio.to_thread, and those threads now all immediately queue on a 4-slot semaphore. The parallelism was un-blocked and then re-bottlenecked one layer down. No launcher sets PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, so every run to date has used the default of 4.

Probe scripts left at /tmp/probe_tokens.py, /tmp/probe_judge.py, /tmp/probe_span.py. No API key printed.

findings.title: Every NLI verdict funnels through a process-global BoundedSemaphore(4) — the verify stage is hard-capped at ~0.9 verdicts/sec no matter what writer concurrency is set to
findings.mechanism: DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4 builds a PROCESS-GLOBAL threading.BoundedSemaphore(4) (judge_concurrency.py:55,137-145). entailment_judge._post_with_total_deadline wraps the ENTIRE judge POST in `with _acquire_judge_slot():` (entailment_judge.py:155). Confirmed call chain: judge() :740 -> _judge_uncached() :765 -> _post_with_total_deadline() :999 -> :155 semaphore. Commit 0615bc5 moved this blocking POST off the event loop into asyncio.to_thread — so now up to 24 writer threads (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 x PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2) run concurrently and ALL of them block on this one 4-slot semaphore. NO launcher (launch_compose_gear_iter1/3/4/5.sh) ever sets PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, so every real run used the default 4. The semaphore was added for a DIFFERENT workload (the per-claim credibility burst that 429-stormed friendli); the compose writer-verify path inherited it silently. Result: the 48-way parallelism the operator asked for is 4-way at the verify stage.

findings.title: A stalled judge POST holds 1 of only 4 semaphore slots for the FULL 300s deadline — one hang removes 25% of process-wide verify capacity for 5 minutes
findings.mechanism: The `with _acquire_judge_slot():` block at entailment_judge.py:155 wraps the whole POST INCLUDING `fut.result(timeout=total_s)` at :174 — the slot is held for the entire wall-deadline, not just for healthy calls. total_s = PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S, whose CODE default is 150s (entailment_judge.py:134) but which the compose launchers RAISE to 300s (launch_compose_gear_iter3.sh:36, iter4.sh:33, iter5.sh:38). Nobody raised the 4-slot pool to compensate. So the launcher doubled the worst-case slot-hold time against an unchanged 4-slot pool: each hung judge socket now sterilizes 25% of the entire process's verification throughput for a full 5 minutes. With the bounded retry (up to 3 attempts) a single bad claim can hold a quarter of all capacity for ~15 minutes.
findings.evidence:

Each of those held 1 of 4 slots for the full 300s = 1,200 slot-seconds burned on dead sockets = 20 minutes of one-quarter of the process's total verify capacity. Same log also shows 26 writer transport-disconnects and 562 'Connection error, retrying' from openrouter_client, so hung/flaky sockets are common in this environment — this is not a rare path. findings.title: PG_S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP=8000 is a DEAD KNOB — exported by all 9 launchers, read by zero lines of Python; 24% of real spans exceed it
findings.mechanism: Every compose launcher exports PG_S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP=8000, but `grep -rn PG_S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP --include=*.py --include=*.yaml .` returns NOTHING — the only files that mention it are the 9 launch scripts that export it. No Python code reads it. The spans are therefore completely uncapped: abstractive_writer._build_writer_prompt (:566-577) loops over ALL basket members with no length cap and embeds each full direct_quote, and verified_compose._member_global_span (:307-325) returns (idx, idx+len(quote)) i.e. the member's ENTIRE quote, which provenance_generator then slices back out as span_text = direct_quote[tok.start:tok.end] (:2298) and embeds verbatim into every entailment-judge prompt (:2558). The operator believes spans are capped at 8,000 chars. They are not.
findings.evidence:
MEASURED, and I am reporting the honest LOW rating rather than inflating it. Real corpus (/workspace/POLARIS/outputs/s2_hamster_i1/cp2_corpus_snapshot.json, 996 evidence rows):
  direct_quote chars: median=2,527  p90=11,435  p99=11,979  max=24,915
  over 8000 chars (the DEAD cap): 239 / 996 = 24.0%
So the cap, if it were wired, would bite on a quarter of all spans. BUT I probed the actual throughput cost and it is small: judge latency across real spans of 628 / 2,385 / 3,428 / 5,184 prompt tokens measured 4.4s / 4.2s / 4.0s / 4.8s median — FLAT. Prefill is cheap on glm-5.2. This is a config-correctness bug (a knob the operator thinks is protecting them and isn't), not a throughput killer. Worth fixing so the config means what it says; do not expect a speedup from it.
findings.fix_sketch: Either wire the knob (truncate direct_quote to PG_S5_SPAN_CHAR_CAP in _build_writer_prompt and in the span_text slice, being careful that the token offsets stay anchored — a naive truncation would break _member_global_span's substring-locate contract and fail-closed the member), or DELETE the export from all 9 launchers so the config stops lying. Deleting is the honest low-risk option given the measured throughput impact is ~zero.

Slot-hold per attempt is bounded at exactly one PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S; there is no continuous 900s hold.
2. The path is barely reached. The SYMPTOM (judge runs near-serial) is real, but the claim mislocates the CAUSE. The finding names verified_compose.py:3325's serial for-loop and asserts "hard concurrency ceiling is therefore 2 = PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS." Both the mechanism and the ceiling are wrong, proven three ways:

1) THE REAL CEILING IS A GLOBAL SEMAPHORE (=4), NOT THE SECTION LOOP. Every entailment_judge POST passes through acquire_judge_slot() in src/polaris_graph/llm/entailment_judge.py:155 (_post_with_total_deadline), which acquires a PROCESS-GLOBAL threading.BoundedSemaphore defined in src/polaris_graph/llm/judge_concurrency.py:106 with DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4 (PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, unset in every launch_*.sh and run_s5_i3.py). This cap is shared across ALL judge sources (compose, pre-pass, credibility, semantic-conflict) and is orthogonal to sections. The claim's "ceiling 2" is measurably false: sweeping /workspace/compose_wt/logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl gives max concurrent entailment_judge intervals = 3 (88s spent at concurrency 3), which is impossible if PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2 were the ceiling. Judges DO run across the serial-loop boundary; they are bounded by the 4-slot semaphore.

2) THE SERIAL COMPOSE LOOP IS NOT THE DOMINANT JUDGE-POST SOURCE. On the production path (launch_compose_gear_iter*.sh set PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER=1 / PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1), the expensive NLI POSTs happen in the CONCURRENT abstractive_pre_pass (abstractive_writer.py:924, semaphore=PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY, each basket in its own to_thread). _pre_pass_one_basket runs _draft_passes_wrapper, which verifies the writer draft with the SAME wrapper object (_vc_writer_verify) the compose loop uses on the SAME sentences/scoped_pool. Because the judge_verdict_cache is default-ON (judge_verdict_cache.py:40, PG_JUDGE_VERDICT_CACHE=1) and judge() returns cached (verdict,reason) BEFORE any POST (entailment_judge.py:756-758), the serial for-loop at verified_compose.py:2221 re-verification is a CACHE HIT that issues no POST. So parallelizing :3325 would move almost no real judge work.

3) THE MEASURED ASYMMETRY IS EXPLAINED BY THE SEMAPHORE + ARRIVAL RATE, NOT THE LOOP. Splitting the ledger at the await-fix (14:23): generate concurrency rose 2.85 -> 8.56 (writer LLM bypasses the judge slot, max 40 in flight); entailment_judge stayed ~1 (0.98 -> 0.89) with post-fix max concurrency = 2 while the cap is 4 — i.e. the semaphore is NOT EVEN BINDING. Judges stay ~serial because writers take 60-121s each while judges take 8.6s, so at any instant only a couple of the 12 concurrent baskets are in their brief verify phase. That is an arrival-rate effect, not a structural section-loop cap of 2.

The finding sends the team to the wrong file and the wrong fix: making verified_compose.py:3325 async/parallel would achieve nothing (compose-loop judges are cache hits; the real POSTs are already concurrent under a 4-slot global cap that isn't binding). The actual levers are PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY (judge_concurrency.py) and the writer-bound judge arrival rate — a materially different mechanism than claimed.
result.real_impact: The judge-is-near-serial phenomenon is real (post-fix achieved judge concurrency 0.89, mean 8.6s, judge = ~5610 vs ~2556 generate calls in the ledger), so the judge side genuinely dominates a large share of wall clock and is worth attacking. But the SPECIFIC lever named is inert. Post-fix data: 481 judge calls, max concurrency 2 against a cap of 4 — the section loop is not the binding constraint and neither is the semaphore; throughput is limited by how fast the slow (60-121s) concurrent writers feed sentences to the ~8.6s judge. Parallelizing verified_compose.py:3325 yields ~0 gain because those calls are verdict-cache hits. The productive knobs are PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY (raise above 4, currently default/unset) AND reducing writer latency / decoupling judge arrival so the 4-slot cap can actually fill — not touching the verified_compose for-loop.
