findings.title: NLI/entailment judge path is capped at 4 concurrent (PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY default=4) and is NOT gated by the writer's global Semaphore(16) — the ceiling once serialization is fixed
findings.mechanism: Every entailment POST goes through acquire_judge_slot() -> a process-global threading.BoundedSemaphore(4). The writer async calls use a SEPARATE asyncio.Semaphore(PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16) in providers/llm_provider.py and never touch the judge slot. So the two OpenRouter paths have independent, unequal caps. Today the 4-slot cap is moot (serial caller only ever holds 1), but it becomes the binding limit the moment Findings 1/2 are fixed to fan out verification — verification would parallelize to 4, not 12/16.
findings.evidence: Sampler read judge BoundedSemaphore._value: sat at 3 (=1 in use) essentially the whole run, dipping to 4-free/0-in-use only in inter-basket gaps — never below 0-free, confirming the 4-cap was never approached because the caller is serial. Slate does not set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, so default 4 is active.
findings.fix_sketch: When enabling concurrent phase-2 verification, also raise PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY to match the intended writer concurrency (e.g. 12-16), and consider unifying the judge under the same global LLM semaphore for one coherent account-wide cap.

findings.title: Rate limiting (429s) and API errors are NOT currently a throughput cap in this account/window
findings.mechanism: Every OpenRouter POST (writer + judge) returned HTTP 200; no 429 backoff sleeps, no retries, no crashes were triggered during the run, so none of the wall-clock is retry/backoff. The slowness is pure serialization, not scarcity.
findings.evidence: HTTP_429_count=0 of 126 POSTs; status_codes {200:21} writer, {200:105} judge. NOTE/CONTAMINATION CAVEAT: the root-owned run_s5_i3.py PID 966951 was ALIVE the entire time (ELAPSED 02:47:18, --cap-primary 0, same OpenRouter account) — yet I still saw zero 429s, so account contention was not biting in this window; a heavier concurrent load could differ. The absolute req/s (0.17) is therefore a floor set by serialization, not by the provider.
findings.fix_sketch: No action on rate limiting now; re-measure 429 rate AFTER the serialization fixes raise in-flight concurrency, since that is when account QPS could start to bind.

findings.title: ~16% of wall is local/gap time between serial judge calls (redraft bookkeeping / shingle+dedup CPU), secondary to the serialization
findings.mechanism: Between consecutive judge POSTs the single-threaded phase-2 loop does local CPU work (sentence splitting, source-shingle construction, span-dedup, repair-loop state) with no LLM call in flight, adding dead time on top of the serial judge latency.
findings.evidence: Occupancy: neither(async=0 AND sync=0)=16.06% of samples (~118s of the 735s run) — visible as the sync=0 stretches at t=118-124, 222-238, 484-500, 710-728 in the concurrency timeline. both=1.16% (judge and writer rarely overlap).
findings.fix_sketch: Minor next to Findings 1-3; once verification is parallelized this local time overlaps with in-flight judge calls and largely disappears. Revisit only if it remains visible after fan-out.

result.notes: Method: monkeypatched httpx.AsyncClient.send (writer path) and httpx.Client.send (judge path) to record per-POST timing/status/in-flight; a 0.25s sampler thread logged async/sync in-flight + both semaphore _values; a heartbeat coroutine measured event-loop starvation. Ran the REAL run_s5_i3.main() in my worktree /home/polaris/wt/compose on section 0, cap-primary=6, timeout 1100s (completed in 735s). Probe: /tmp/probe_compose.py, /tmp/run_probe.sh; log: /tmp/probe.log. An earlier cap-12 run I accidentally killed with pkill still yielded consistent numbers (writer avg concurrency 0.46, max 3; 0.15 POST/s; 0 429s). Headline: the missing-await fix made ONLY the ~16s prepass concurrent; the dominant phase-2 NLI verification (91% of wall) is still a synchronous, on-the-event-loop, one-at-a-time per-basket loop — this is the remaining throughput killer, and it also blocks the loop (heartbeat gap 631s) so cross-section parallelism (MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2) cannot help either. CONTAMINATION: root job PID 966951 alive on same OpenRouter account throughout, but 0 429s observed so structural findings are unaffected.

result.reason: Tried to kill the claim by measuring a much larger sample than the investigator's 126 POSTs: the live root-owned compose run's own log (/workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log), 4639 OpenRouter POSTs over the full 08:33-15:58 (~7.4h) window on the SAME account. Result confirms the claim rather than refuting it: 4627x HTTP 200, 12x HTTP 429 (0.26%), zero other status codes, zero ERROR/judge_error/error_unexpected lines. Every 429 is recovered ('rate-limit backoff 15.0s' then a successful retry). Total backoff cost = 12 x 15.0s = 180s = 0.68% of run wall-clock. The backoff is time.sleep (entailment_judge.py:1166/1209) but runs inside asyncio.to_thread (post-0615bc5), and the POST semaphore slot is explicitly released during backoff (entailment_judge.py:151), so one judge's 429 wait blocks neither the event loop nor its 11 sibling judges — no amplification. The claim that 429s/API errors are NOT a throughput cap (impact LOW) holds up under a larger, independent measurement; I could not find a mechanism that makes it a cap.
result.real_impact: Rate limiting costs ~180s of thread-local backoff over ~7.4h of runtime (0.68% of wall-clock), fully recovered (all 12 429s retried to 200), non-amplifying (semaphore released during backoff, sleep is off the event loop). Negligible vs the serialization/latency costs; not a throughput cap in this account/window.

result.reason: The claim's code path (dedup/shingle/span-dedup in _compose_section_per_basket, verified_compose.py:3323+) is real and reached, but its measured CPU cost is ~500x smaller than claimed. A probe at full 346-unit scale times the entire dedup+shingle+Jaccard-clustering work at ~0.25s total (5.78ms per-unit bookkeeping + 6ms shingles + 213ms worst-case O(N^2) clustering), i.e. ~0.03% of the 735s run, not 16%/118s. The repair loop between judge POSTs (verify_fn at :2222/:2583) is pure string/set manipulation with no heavy pass. The measured 118s "neither" time is real but misattributed: with writer_fn a precomputed dict lookup, phase-B _compose_section_per_basket runs judge POSTs serially on the event loop, so the neither-gaps are the serial judge structure plus each judge's own local/network overhead (span resolution, TLS, framing inside the entailment judge/verify_sentence_provenance — a different file), which is a facet of the serialization root cause, not CPU in the dedup passes. Optimizing the named code recovers ~0.25s.
result.real_impact: If it somehow survived: eliminating ALL dedup/shingle/clustering CPU in verified_compose recovers ~0.25s over a 346-basket run (~0.03% of 735s), essentially zero. The claimed 118s is dominated by the serial judge call structure and per-judge local/network overhead, not this code.

result.reason: Could not kill it; confirmed by code + their measurement. (1) Reached in production: all launch_compose_gear_*.sh set PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1 + PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER=1, so _run_section takes the abstractive branch and _compose_section_per_basket (verified_compose.py:3262, loop :3325) is the primary body producer. (2) It is a bare SYNC call — multi_section_generator.py:5468/5514 call it with NO await and NO to_thread inside `async def _run_section` (:5182). (3) verify_fn (_vc_writer_verify -> make_writer_verify_fn -> verify_sentence_provenance -> _get_judge().judge() -> _post_with_total_deadline -> fut.result(), entailment_judge.py:172) is a BLOCKING single-worker POST executed on the event-loop thread; no fan-out, strictly serial per sentence per basket. (4) The 0615bc5 missing-await fix was in the PRE-PASS (_draft_passes_wrapper wrapped in await asyncio.to_thread at abstractive_writer.py:833/912); phase-2 is untouched, so that fix bought nothing here — matches the claim. Refutation attempts failed: section concurrency (Semaphore=3 default at :9937) does NOT parallelize this because the bare sync call blocks the whole event loop through every ~5.5s POST, so phase-2 serializes LOOP-WIDE across sections, not just within one — worse than stated. The default-ON verdict cache (entailment_judge.py:756) does not save it empirically: the investigator measured 105 real HTTP-200 judge POSTs busy 673s in phase-2 (plausibly because phase-2 re-resolves spans, changing the cache key vs the pre-pass). Not dominated by a bigger cost: judge busy 673.0s vs writer 20.4s of 735.2s wall (91.5%), sync-inflight pinned at ~1 for 82.95% of wall.
result.real_impact: In the measured section: judge verification is 673.0s of 735.2s wall-clock (91.5%), running at achieved concurrency ~0.92 (effectively 1) via 105 serial ~5.5s POSTs. Because _compose_section_per_basket is a bare sync call blocking the event loop, this serialization is loop-wide, so neither PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=3 nor the missing-await fix helps it. Fanning the judge out (or offloading the loop per-section to worker threads so section concurrency actually applies) cuts the judge-bound phase ~3-12x (673s->~168s at 4, ~56s at 12), consistent with their extrapolation. Caveat: the full 346-basket projection assumes uniform judge-POST density across baskets, which I could not independently verify; the confirmed part is the per-section serialization and ~90% wall-clock-at-concurrency-1.

result.reason: Could not refute. Every load-bearing element of the claim is confirmed by reading the real code and by a measured probe. (1) judge_concurrency.py DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4 and resolve_max_concurrency() returns 4 when unset; a 12-thread probe admitted a PEAK of exactly 4 concurrent judge slots. (2) entailment_judge.py:155 wraps every judge POST in _acquire_judge_slot() — a process-global threading.BoundedSemaphore, distinct from the writer's PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM asyncio semaphore in src/providers/llm_provider.py:79. Independent, unequal caps: confirmed. (3) Path is definitely reached: the compose slate (launch_compose_gear_iter3/4/5.sh) sets PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT=enforce, and abstractive_writer.py:1136 hard-errors unless enforce, so _get_judge().judge() fires on every accepted sentence (provenance_generator.py:2558). (4) The slate does NOT set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY (only scripts/dr_benchmark/run_gate_b.py sets it, to 2 — a different harness), so default 4 is live. (5) The judge model is z-ai/glm-5.2 — the SAME expensive reasoning model as the writer — so each of the ~2-per-sentence judge POSTs is comparable in cost to a writer call, making the 4-cap plausibly co-dominant/dominant post-serialization, not negligible. I tried to refute on 'entailment off by default' (module default IS off, but slate forces enforce), on 'env overridden' (not in the compose slate), on 'dominated by writer' (aggregate judge work ~ writer work due to glm-5.2 + double verification), and on 'not reached' (it is) — all failed. Claim survives.
result.real_impact: Post-serialization, verification fan-out is hard-capped at 4 concurrent glm-5.2 judge POSTs regardless of writer concurrency (12) or PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM (16-30) — measured peak=4 with 12 waiters. Judge is invoked on every accepted sentence, ~2 passes (pre-pass proposal + authoritative compose gate), model z-ai/glm-5.2 at ~6-40s/call. Rough 346-basket aggregate: writer ~69k s-work at 12-16 wide ≈ 4.3-5.8k s wall; judge ~60k s-work at 4 wide ≈ 15k s wall. If pipelined, the judge 4-cap is the binding wall and raising it toward 12-16 would cut judge wall ~3x (~15k→~5k s), i.e. it becomes the dominant ceiling once serialization is fixed. Caveat on the REMEDY (not the finding): the _live_run log shows the judge path 429-storming the SAME OpenRouter account; naively raising the cap risks re-triggering the account-QPS 429s the de-storm cap was added to prevent, so realized speedup depends on shared-account headroom.

result.reason:
Every refutation angle failed. (1) Reachability: the sync path IS reached at runtime — probe.log shows section 0 entering it with PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY ON. (2) Not guarded elsewhere: _compose_section_per_basket (verified_compose.py:3262) is called bare (no await, no to_thread) at multi_section_generator.py:5468/5514 from async _run_section; its own docstring at :4938 admits it "runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop," and the redraft closure _make_group_redraft_fn (:4993) blocks the loop on fut.result(). (3) Same-loop parallelism: _run_legacy_bounded (:10115) runs `async with sem: await _run_section` under asyncio.Semaphore + asyncio.gather — sections share ONE event loop, so a sync block freezes all siblings. (4) Impact is real and NOT dominated: probe.log records max_gap_s=631.09 spanning t≈78.8s→709.9s — the loop did not tick for the entire compose phase (86% of the 734s section), while the awaited pre-pass (78s) and awaited strict_verify (~24s tail, to_thread) tick fine. My strongest counter — that the judge_verdict_cache would make the re-verify cache-warm and cheap — is defeated because PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY's group-redraft loop makes 9 fresh blocking glm writer calls + ~87 NLI POSTs inside the sync window (uncacheable, all on the loop thread). Minor imprecisions in the claim (dominant cost is the redraft writer calls, not strictly NLI POSTs; the env var is PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS not PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS) do not change the serialization conclusion.</parameter>
<parameter name="real_impact">Measured: the sync _compose_section_per_basket blocks the shared event loop for 631s per section (86% of the 734s single-section probe; pre-pass is only 78s, strict_verify ~24s). Because all sections run on one loop via asyncio.gather + Semaphore(3 default), each section's ~631s compose fully freezes the loop, so the compose phases of a 4-section/346-basket run serialize end-to-end (~4×631s ≈ 42 min that PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS is supposed to overlap but cannot) — and even peer sections' pre-passes cannot interleave during a sibling's compose block. Fix: dispatch _compose_section_per_basket via await asyncio.to_thread(...) at both call sites (:5468, :5514), exactly like the pre-pass fix in commit 0615bc5 and the strict_verify call at :5667 already do. Gated on PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY/PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER being ON (they are, in the measured config and per the code's 'primary body path' comments); with synth_primary OFF the redraft writer calls vanish and cache-warm verification would shrink the block substantially.</parameter>
</invoke>

result:
Note: claude-opus-4-8[1m] (the safety classifier) was unavailable when reviewing this subagent's work. Please carefully verify the subagent's actions and output before acting on them.

Spot-checks confirm the two load-bearing facts at HEAD in /home/polaris/wt/compose: `_compose_section_per_basket` is still called bare (no await/to_thread) at src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514, and `DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4` at src/polaris_graph/llm/judge_concurrency.py:55 with `PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY` set in zero launch scripts (grep exit 1).

WHY COMPOSE IS SLOW — SYNTHESIS OF SURVIVING FINDINGS

1. RANKED CAUSES (real cost at 346-basket scale)

#1 — The compose phase itself still runs ON the event loop. [MEASURED, dominant]
src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514: `async def _run_section` calls the sync `_compose_section_per_basket` (verified_compose.py:3262) with no await and no to_thread. Inside it, every per-sentence verify blocks on `fut.result()` and — worse — the SYNTH_PRIMARY redraft (`_make_group_redraft_fn`, multi_section_generator.py:4994-4996) blocks the loop on a FULL glm-5.2 writer call, up to 330s per firing, up to 2x per failed basket. 0615bc5 fixed this bug class in the pre-pass only; this is the same bug one layer up, unfixed at HEAD.
Measured: heartbeat probe showed the loop did not tick for 631 consecutive seconds during one section's compose (86% of a 734s section); instrumented section 0: judge busy 673.0s of 735.2s wall at inflight pinned to 1; iter4 log: section 3 drafted 0/13 baskets in 1700s while section 2 held the loop, then 13/13 in 315s once released; section 0 total 3266s of which only 591s (pre-pass) was parallel — 82% of the section at concurrency 1. This is the reason PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12, PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16, and PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS are all inert during compose. Cost: the majority of the measured ~7.4h iter4 wall.

#2 — Process-global NLI judge semaphore hard-capped at 4. [MEASURED, becomes binding the moment #1 is fixed]
judge_concurrency.py:55 DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4; entailment_judge.py:155 wraps every judge POST in `_acquire_judge_slot()`; PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY is set in no launcher (verified again just now). The judge uses its own httpx client, so PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 never applies to it. Measured: probes admit peak exactly 4 with 24/48 waiters (6.0x slowdown vs unbounded); a real ledger session ran 2170 judge calls at achieved concurrency 3.94 for 8208s. At ~4.4-5.5s/verdict and ~1000-2000 verdicts per 346-basket run, cap 4 puts a ~19-46 min floor under the verify leg; cap 12 cuts that ~3x. Today it is masked by #1 (a serial caller never needs more than 1 slot).

#3 — sentence_repair: awaited one-at-a-time repair loop + sync verify on the loop. [MEASURED counts, MEDIUM]
sentence_repair.py:408/:428/:519. ~90-99 repair attempts per run across ~10-11 sections, each a serialized glm-5.2 reasoning call plus a loop-blocking NLI POST. Bounded per-section (cap 10), does not scale with baskets; order of 1-2 min of loop freeze plus serialized repair tails. Fix after #1/#2.

#4 — coherence_rewrite_section sync verify in async loop. [CODE-READ only, LOW]
section_polish.py:271-283. Real bug, same class, but fires only on changed sentences, once per section — tens of seconds per run at most. Refuter downgraded from HIGH.

NOT a cause — 429s / API errors. [MEASURED] 12 of 4639 POSTs were 429 (0.26%), all recovered, 180s total backoff = 0.68% of wall. The original "429 is the ceiling" thesis remains dead — with one caveat under gap (5).

2. THE OPERATOR'S THREE HYPOTHESES, BY NAME

- CONTEXT TOO LARGE? NO, on current evidence. Judge latency measured flat across span sizes (median 4.4-5.5s); single writer calls measure 7-11s live. Nothing in eight lanes surfaced an unbounded prompt-concatenation mechanism. Caveat: nobody actually counted prompt tokens per call — this is a "no confirmed mechanism + flat latency" no, not a token-count no.
- TOKEN BUDGET? NO — the opposite. No truncation finding survived. Budgets are enormous (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS=131072, reasoning 65536), which makes each *serialized* redraft call take 30-78s. Budget is not starving compose; oversized reasoning budgets amplify the serialization cost of #1.
- CRASHES? NO. Zero errors/exceptions in the measured windows; all 12 429s retried to 200; no abandonments except the pre-pass wall-deadline event, which was itself CAUSED by #1 (a starved sibling section), not a crash.

The real answer to "what else is capping compose" is: the same missing-await bug class, twice more (compose phase and its redraft path), plus a 4-slot judge semaphore inherited from a different workload.

3. THE ONE THING
Wrap both call sites in `await asyncio.to_thread(_compose_section_per_basket, ...)` at multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514 (functools.partial for kwargs). This unfreezes the loop: sections genuinely overlap, sibling pre-passes stop starving, and the redraft's inner `asyncio.run` (already on its own worker thread) becomes correct off-loop. Expected speedup: the measured runs spent ~82-86% of section wall at concurrency 1 because of this; restoring 2-3-way section overlap plus intra-run pipelining is a realistic 3-6x on total wall. Strongly recommended rider (one line, zero code): export PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=12 in the launcher — otherwise the to_thread fix immediately hits the 4-slot judge wall and you capture only part of the win.

4. HONEST WALL-CLOCK AFTER THE TOP FIX
Baseline: 346 baskets took ~7.4h (iter4). With the to_thread fix ALONE: verify floor = ~1000-2000 judge POSTs x ~5s / 4 slots ≈ 21-42 min, plus writer/redraft legs at 12-16 wide ≈ 10-25 min, partially pipelined — estimate ~1 to 1.5 hours. With the judge cap also raised to 12: verify floor drops to ~7-15 min — estimate ~30-60 minutes. This rests on four assumptions: (a) judge-POST density ~3-5/basket holds across all 346 baskets (measured on sections 0-3 only); (b) the redraft/verify-failure rate stays at observed levels (not measured at full scale); (c) no 429 storm at true 12-16-way concurrency (429s were only measured under serialization — see gaps); (d) per-call latencies (judge ~5s, writer 7-30s) hold. Treat 30-90 min as the honest band, not a point estimate.

5. WHAT WE DID NOT ESTABLISH
- Verify-failure/redraft frequency at 346-basket scale. The 330s-per-redraft path is confirmed live, but how many of 346 baskets trigger it is unmeasured; it swings the estimate by tens of minutes.
- 429 behavior at real concurrency. "429 is not a cap" was measured at ~0.17 req/s effective — i.e., while serialized. At genuine 12-16-way on one OpenRouter account it may bind; must be re-measured after the fix.
- The verdict-cache contradiction. One measured lane saw 105 real phase-2 judge POSTs (cache misses); another verdict says compose-phase verifies are cache hits from the pre-pass. The cache-key mismatch (span re-resolution?) is unexplained; it determines whether phase-2 verify cost is ~0 or ~600s/section post-fix.
- Writer-vs-judge dominance post-fix. Whether generation (16-way pool) or verify (judge pool) is the binding stage after both fixes is unresolved; they may co-bottleneck.
- Actual prompt token counts. The "context not too large" answer is inferential; no one measured tokens-per-call across basket sizes.
- No end-to-end A/B of the fixed build at 346 baskets exists yet. Everything above extrapolates from 4-15-basket probes and the iter4 log.
